Procedures in Case of Breaches or Alleged Breaches of Confidentiality 69 The list of Primary sources 71 List of Secondary sources 73 Introduction The Chemical Weapons Convention (hereinafter CWC / the Convention) is an international agreement on the control of chemical weapons that prohibits the production, stockpiling and use of chemical weapons and any chemical toxic substances . Based on the provisions of the CWC, the field of chemical safety encompasses both political issues at the level of governments of states and issues of interaction of these governments with the chemical industry  —  it is the representatives of the chemical industry that are one of the main  suppliers of chemical materials, their precursors and chemical weapons, as well as the main by the customer of the CWC, whose conditions affect the activities of chemical enterprises . In recent years, more and more information has appeared about industrial accidents, the use of chemical weapons or other toxic chemicals against the military and civilian population. The risks of illegal production and storage of chemical weapons and their transfer to third parties, such as terrorist organizations, are still high, despite the fact that the CWC provides for complete disarmament, the destruction of all accumulated stocks of chemical weapons, and a ban on their re-emergence. Accordingly, the chemical industry is the first suspect in violation of the terms of the CWC. The terms of the Convention provide for such a concept as a “chemical facility” . However, the problems arising in the interaction of the governments of the CWC States Parties with chemical facilities do not cover one or several specific enterprises but the entire chemical industry, including going beyond the national level. The chemical industry is directly and indirectly related to the field of chemical safety. Under the terms of the Convention, there is a clear definition of chemical security and chemical safety. The CWC defines chemical safety as measures to prevent the inadvertent release of toxic chemicals into the environment and to mitigate exposure if such events occur . At the same time, chemical safety includes such disciplines as occupational safety, public safety, process safety, environmental safety, consumer safety and transport safety . Many of these disciplines are also addressed by different international conventions and a number of international organizations. In turn, chemical security refers to measures to prevent the deliberate release of toxic chemicals and to mitigate exposure if such events occur . In a broader context, it also includes a policy to prevent attempts to acquire toxic chemicals or precursors of chemical weapons. The chemical industry is actively developing both from the point of view of the scientific and technical sphere, and from the point of view of involvement in political processes of states, including in the international arena within the framework of the OPCW. The  goal and main research question  of this work is to identify the political problems and aspects that arise in the cooperation of the OPCW with the chemical industry under the CWC regime. Based on the set goal, the following  tasks  were identified: 1. To outline the role that the chemical industry played in the initial CWC negotiations and how this role has evolved. 2. To examine what the deepening interaction of the chemical industry with OPCW representatives has led to. 3. To identify the channels and mechanisms of interaction between the chemical industry and national authorities at the state and international levels. 4. To highlight the political problems that arise in the interaction of the chemical industry and the governments of the States Parties within the framework of the OPCW. 5. To analyze the extent to which the OPCW activities are complete and sufficient for the further development of cooperation with the chemical industry. 6. To determine what the chemical industry can do within the OPCW to strengthen the CWC regime. In this way, the  relevance  of the presented topic lies in the fact that against the background of increasing  geopolitical  differences between the CWC States Parties, problems in the interaction of the chemical industry with the OPCW representatives are becoming more acute. The chemical industry, as one of the parties involved in the implementation of the Chemical Weapons Convention, is interested in solving these problems and strengthening the Convention regime. The  scientific novelt y of the study lies in the fact that this work covers the cooperation of the chemical industry with government officials on political issues both at the state level and at the level of the international community. At the same time, the work provides an extensive analysis of the role of the chemical industry in solving political problems and strengthening the CWC regime. Based on the analysis of the source base, it can be concluded that the cooperation of the chemical industry with government officials within the CWC at the international level has been little researched. It is important to note that the analysis of regulatory legal acts (the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons , reports and agreements on the results of meetings, conferences and meetings of subordinate bodies of the OPCW , etc.), personal interviews with direct or indirect participants in the negotiations on the CWC played an important role in the study (Dr. Ralph Trapp , Ambassador Batsanov Sergey Borisovich ). Among the source base, another important group is the official sites of chemical companies (for example, Icmesa Company ), and the official sites of the Chemical Industry Associations of the CWC States Parties . This group of sources made it possible to form a clearer vision of the positions of representatives of the chemical industry, primarily regarding the issues of combating common threats and challenges, as well as how they see the future of ensuring the CWC regime. Based on the analysis of sources and literature, it can be concluded that most of the work is related to the technical issues of interaction between the chemical industry and the national authorities and the OPCW. Some researchers and authors pay more attention to the role of the OPCW, ignoring the changing position of the chemical industry and its influence on some political processes within states, which accordingly affects the decision-making process in the international community. In addition, the studied literature and sources overlook the fact that the chemical industry has gone through certain stages of development in order to be able to fully interact with the national governments of their states.  The topic of the presented work was tested in the framework of the international research competition of the South Korean Institute of Advanced Technology (KAIST) and was published in the annual collection of this university in March 2021 . The presented study consists of three main chapters, the collective conclusions of which represent the answer to the main research question of the entire study. The first chapter presents a vision of the role and place of the chemical industry representatives in the initial negotiations for the CWC in the 1990s and what the main issues related to the chemical industry were raised on the agenda. The second chapter presents an analysis of the current state of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons in the modern realities of international relations, what political problems are facing the chemical industry and the OPCW in the framework of their interaction with each other through certain channels and communication mechanisms. Finally, the third and final chapter explores the potential of the chemical industry to support the CWC regime in the future. Thus, this work provides a comprehensive analysis of the role of the chemical industry in the chemical safety system at the national and international levels, as well as an analysis of the problems and opportunities for the future development of the regime of the Chemical Weapons Convention. Chapter 1. Chemical Industry and the  CWC Negotiation Process Industry is one of the areas related to arms control.  The chemical industry takes the place of one of the main manufacturers and suppliers of chemical weapons. The Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (hereinafter CWC) is directly related to the activities of chemical enterprises. The final version of the CWC text includes the conditions not only for the destruction of existing chemical weapons, but also for the further activities of chemical production facilities. This chapter analyzes the history of the negotiation process. Firstly, the reasons for involving the chemical industry in the CWC negotiations are examined. Secondly, the main tasks and problems that were initially posed to the chemical industry and the States parties to the Convention are highlighted. Thirdly, the consequences of this involvement of the chemical industry at the initial stage of the CWC negotiations are  explored. 1.1. The involvement of the chemical industry in the negotiation process The negotiation process on the development of a Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (CWC) took quite a long time and included numerous summits, as well as consultations of various working groups. At a certain stage in the development of the convention, the question arose “What to do with the chemical industry?” The provisions of the CWC are closely related to the restriction of work and the rearrangement of the chemical industry of the countries participating in the convention. These restrictions had a very strong impact on the work and life of industry , thereby affecting also the internal sphere, primarily the economic component. In the mid-1980s, the issue of chemical disarmament was raised at the Conference on Disarmament. Some European countries (Sweden, USA, Germany, etc.) have proposed that in order to achieve the best results, consultations with representatives of the chemical industry should be considered . The interaction of the chemical industry with national governments during the CWC negotiation process was not initially a given. Their relationship in many countries was rather antagonistic during the 1970-s  —  1980-s , as governments introduced increasingly stringent safety and environmental regulations in the industry, especially after the release of hazardous chemicals in Seveso  and Bhopal . The situation changed in the 1980-s after the use of chemical weapons by Iraq against Iran and other countries , as well as Libya’s attempts to acquire CW   —  because of that the chemical industry was under attack from negative public opinion. These accidents with the use of chemical weapons led to the fact that a shadow, a black mark, fell on the entire chemical industry as a threat to all mankind. Accordingly, national chemical plants suffered from budget losses and other negative consequences of such deposition.  Contrary to initial information from the media that Iraqi chemical weapons were supplied by the USSR, it turned out that a significant part of the precursor chemicals and production equipment were obtained from open commercial sources . For example, surveys conducted in the United States during that period showed that the chemical industry was significantly inferior to the tobacco industry.  The same situation occurred in other countries. It has become important for the chemical industry not only to maintain its position in society, but also not to become the “enemy of the people number 1”, who will be blamed for all the troubles associated with the chemical weapons without trial or investigation. Thus, in the middle of 1980-s, the situation with the verification of the chemical industry became a problem for national authorities and chemical industry too, therefore, representatives of the chemical industry began to pay more and more attention to participation in the negotiations on the prohibition of chemical weapons, thereby seeking to restore their reputation and good name. Whereas previously the interaction between government and industry was largely passive  —  governments sought technical advice from industry representatives, then in the 1980-s industry took on a more active role, effectively lobbying negotiators and putting forward its own proposals at the national and international levels.  Industry associations in some Western countries, such as the USA, the United Kingdom and Germany, have set up working groups to oversee ongoing negotiations. These groups were consulted at the national level by the government agency that coordinated the negotiating positions  —  this was often the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. The American chemical industry was one of the first to pay attention to the negotiations on chemical weapons. Representatives of the United States Industrial Chemists' Association  —  UIA (later the Association of Chemical Manufacturers  —  CMA, now the American Chemicals Council  —  ACC) and the Arms Control and Disarmament Agency (ACDA) first met to discuss the CWC in 1978, and a constant informal dialogue began in 1979 . Already in the mid 1980-s, the Chemical Producers Association established a Working Group on the Disarmament of Chemical Weapons that met every month and included meetings with government officials. Similar consultative mechanisms between industry and government have been established in other countries, for example, in Great Britain in the mid 1980-s. The government and chemical industry came together to discuss the projected CWC in the Government and Industry Working Group; in Germany, a kind of working group was created between the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the Ministry of Defense, other governmental entities and with the participation of the Germany chemical industry association VCI . In some cases, this link eventually became stronger, for example, in Germany, an industry representative was appointed a full member of the German delegations to the CD negotiations at the end of the negotiations in 1992  —  1993 . Meetings of several national and international chemical industry associations were held periodically during the period of 1990-s. The purpose of those meetings was the discussion of how chemical industry associations can be involved in the negotiations, in what issues their participation is more important, and how to construct the interaction between national authorities and chemical industry representatives.  Among the associations,  can be highlighted the activities of such as the British Chemical Industry Association (CIA), the European Council of the Chemical Industry (CEFIC), the Western European Association representing EU members, the CMA from the USA, the Canadian Chemical Manufacturers Association (CCPA) and the Japan Chemical Industry Association (JCIA).   At the international level, the chemical industry focused on Geneva as the main arena and center of the CWC negotiations. At the first stage of interaction, the chemical industry took a wait-and-see attitude but at subsequent stages representatives of the chemical industry showed themselves to be active and interested participants in the negotiations. Since the early 1980-s, unofficial channels have operated through the Pugwash CBW research group, the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI) and several other organizations. They offered forums for discussion and then, in some cases, organized ad hoc case studies on industry verification issues . In the late 1980-s, Sweden, during its second chairmanship of the ad hoc working group of the Conference on Disarmament (CD) on Chemical Weapons, organized the first informal meeting between CD negotiators and industry representatives — at this stage it was just an exchange of opinions and of update information to the industry on the status of the CWC negotiations . So, in July 1987, about 60 chemical industry’s representatives from 32 countries met in Geneva twice: with and without CWC State Parties. Such meetings have become a regular occurrence in Geneva as the negotiation process progresses. In addition, the chemical industry has formed an international coalition in order to more effectively convey its positions and views to States Parties of CD in Geneva. The main participants in this coalition were chemical enterprises in Western Europe (organized under the auspices of CEFIC, the European Chemical Industry Council), Japan (JCIP), Canada (Canadian Chemical Manufacturers Association), Australia (Australian Chemical Industry Council) and the USA (Chemical Manufacturers Association). Industry associations have also worked together through the International Council of Chemical Associations. It took time for the chemical industry to learn how to interact constructively with the negotiators in Geneva. Industry representatives noted that to influence the negotiation process, they need to come to such meetings with a coordinated position on specific issues, and these positions should be available to negotiators in the form of written position papers . This quickly became a standard practice, and negotiators had more and at their disposal more detailed and specific industry positions on issues such as listing / excluding certain chemicals, what inspections should be carried out in chemical plants, what restrictions should be followed in relation to sampling and analysis in chemical plants; safety during inspections and so on . In the period from July 1987 to July 1988, several meetings were held in Geneva between representatives of the chemical industry and delegates of the CD. Further meetings between industry representatives and CD delegates were held in Geneva in 1990 and 1991. This interaction was also a two-way process, with some countries using their chemical industry associations to include more pragmatic proposals in the draft text of the convention. In Canberra, in September 1989, 375 delegates from 66 countries and four international organizations attended the Government Industry Conference on Chemical Weapons Control (GICCW) . About half of the delegations included representatives from the national chemical industry, accounting for about 20% of the participation. Many delegations included representatives from trade unions and  officers of governmental agencies of industry and trade  at this conference, which meant that about 95% of the world's chemical production  facilities were represented . Industry representatives adopted a collective statement, in which, among other things, they expressed their “willingness to actively work with governments to achieve a global ban on chemical weapons, as well as their willingness to add momentum to the negotiation process in Geneva” and declared their “willingness to continue the dialogue with governments to prepare for the entry into force of an effective Chemical Weapons Convention that protects the free and non-discriminatory exchange of chemicals and transfer of technology for economic development and the well-being of all people” . They also agreed to establish the International Chemical Industry Forum, meeting in Geneva, as the focal point for industry participation in the Convention negotiations. At the same time, different governments had their own reasons and motives to involve representatives of the chemical industry in the negotiation process. Sweden  proposed ​​involving the chemical industry in the discussion of some articles of the convention by the governments of the participating countries . Despite the fact that Sweden had a small export volume of its chemical products, the pharmaceutical field played an important role in its national economy. The CWC is making a big imprint on this area of ​​the chemical industry. By involving industry representatives, the Swedish authorities planned to find a consensus on prohibited chemicals and restrictions on the work of enterprises with minimal negative consequences for their production and the country's economy . Another important factor in this interaction was the need to consider the problems of the chemical industry and how they could and should be reflected directly in national positions during negotiations. When considering external and internal factors, two main reasons for such an interest in attracting the chemical industry, primarily from countries with developed industries, namely Japan, the United States and Western European states, were identified : The chemical industry has an effective lobbying mechanism in parliaments to voice concerns about new regulations and legislation and to advocate for their interests  —  governments need to ensure industry participation so that the convention and relevant national implementing laws and regulations go through parliaments. Governments required some technical knowledge in the chemical industry, which they themselves possessed only to a limited extent. This was necessary in order to develop effective (from the government's point of view) trade inspection and control mechanisms that would actually work in industry. Also governments  had some concerns with special interests in areas related to managing the impact of the Chemical Weapons Convention on the international trade in chemicals and related goods, equipment and technologies. Significantly, this was the time of NATO's strategic trade restrictions in areas of economic and military importance, so security considerations could not be separated from economic interests . There has been a combination of traditional reluctance to agree with verification in general and in sensitive areas (military or commercial) in particular, with fears that verification measures (inspections, reporting, etc.) could negatively affect an industry of strategic importance. In Russia and the EU, industrial facilities and industrial elements directly related to the CW program have been integrated into the national economy and enterprises and into large industrial complexes, for example, the production and military complex . Thus, there was a clear need to protect the CW program from outside interference and influence. Considering that these were all state-owned enterprises, sectoral and state interests still could not be separated. In many developing countries, this was more of a long-term development issue: the chemicals and chemical technology industry and trade might not have been under the direct control of governments, but governments had a strategic interest in protecting industrial and agricultural development and trade from external interference, control and anything that might hinder participation in the global economy. The chemical industry has been and remains a strategic engine for economic growth in developing countries . Therefore, they eagerly listened to the voices of industry representatives that they could use to oppose overly strict or intrusive measures regarding verification, trade controls and national regulations. Those non-aligned countries that initiated chemical weapons development programs and that participated in the CD negotiations (for example, India or Iran) have tried to strengthen their defensive position for both their public and private industries, minimizing the risks of information disclosure for their own states on CW programs. This effectively meant that they also tried to minimize the intrusiveness and scope of industry auditing and trade controls, for example in the form of generic (the implementation of measures that include a holistic verification of both individual materials and technical equipment, finished products, and other indicators) versus sample (the seizure of those substances that are used at a chemical enterprise in order to establish their quantity and whether there are any substances among them that are prohibited by the terms of the CWC) verification concepts . Significantly, countries such as Iran and India did not acknowledge that they had a program for the development of chemical weapons, even within the country  —  the very existence of that programs was still a secret and verification could reveal that programs, and the industry's fears of an invasion through verification could be used to contain the very big ambitions of chemical productions. The involvement of representatives of the chemical industry has become for some governments a way of some kind of control  over the negotiation process, as well as the decision-making process. At the same time, in countries with state-owned industry, it was easy to involve industry representatives in the CWC negotiations: industry representatives were integrated into all internal structures created to coordinate policy and negotiation positions between various ministries and government agencies; often, the overall coordination function was performed by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. This informal public-private partnership was mutually beneficial for both parties. Governments received technical advice from industry and a deeper understanding of their problems, and industry became more aware of the difficult political side of any negotiation. A key factor in the successful participation of the chemical industry in the negotiations of the Convention was the facilitation of such engagement by non-governmental organizations, in particular the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI) and the Pugwash Conferences on Science and World Affairs. Both organizations have previously conducted detailed studies related to the verification of the chemical industry and recognized the importance of industry participation in this context . In the period from 1985 to 1988, SIPRI conducted a number of meetings and studies, the results of which were published. During this time, Pugwash, in turn, also invited very often representatives of the chemical industry to various meetings and seminars, which one of the participants describes as a “‎valuable addition” to other more formal meetings . Describing such meetings, Will Carpenter says, “‎NGOs were able to bring together key government and industry officials in a forum where frank dialogue took place on key technical and operational issues” . In this way, a series of incidents involving chemicals and chemical weapons have become a kind of catalyst for the interest of the chemical industry and its intensification of participation in political processes. After considering the reasons why the chemical industry was involved in the process of developing the conditions of the Chemical Weapons Convention, it can be noted that this interaction was beneficial to both parties.  On the one hand, for the chemical industry:  Protecting its interests  Ensuring confidentiality  The ability to ‎control the level of verification at their enterprises  Improving working conditions  On the other hand, for the governments of the CWC States Parties: A thorough study of the technical side of the CWC  The development of verification mechanisms that would work not only on paper, but also in practice  Promotion of their views among the population and in Parliaments through the lobbying positions of representatives of the chemical industry  This involvement of the chemical industry allowed governments to receive support from the public and to establish close contact and relatively trusting relations with society, and also showed some transparency of the policy and diplomacy pursued by the government of states. Participation in the development of the technical component of the CWC gave the representatives of the chemical industry the opportunity to clear their good name in the eyes of the public and strengthen their position at the national level. The chemical industry understood that the Convention would hit their businesses very hard, thereby undermining the country's economy, and many businesses could go bankrupt in the future.  The strengthening of the interaction of the chemical industry with national authorities within the framework of the OPCW allowed the chemical industry to master the mechanisms of interaction in the political sphere, for example, the preparation of written appeals, statements, etc. This contributed to the fact that representatives of the chemical industry learned to more clearly formulate their positions and interests. Also, thanks to the increased interest of the chemical industry in the negotiations on the CWC terms, channels were created for constant interaction between the chemical industry and national governments, which brought the development of the chemical industry to a new level. In this way, initially, the main reason why the chemical industry was involved in the negotiations on the CWC was to protect its corporate interests. After the incidents in Seveso and Bhopal, and with Iran and Libya, the chemical industry was blacklisted by the entire civilian population. Being one of the active participants in the development of the CWC, the chemical industry showed people a desire to live in a world without chemical weapons and to secure its activities for the entire civilian population, placing it under some control of the governments of their states. 1.2. The main chemical industry-related issues in the CWC The involvement of the chemical industry in the negotiation process had its own reasons and goals on the part of the governments of the participating countries. Representatives of the chemical industry were interested in that interaction both at the national and international levels. Due to the fact that the Chemical Weapons Convention in one way or another affects the activities of chemical enterprises, it was important to reflect  as much as possible all the nuances, for example, verification system, types of inspections and its frequency, the transition from the goals of military production to non-military ones, the sampling of chemicals and their precursors prohibited by the CWC terms, etc. In 1994 some observers of the negotiations on the CWC noted that it is the activities not prohibited by the Convention that will represent a rather complex combination of several important areas of state life: science, technology, trade, and politics . The final verification regime, which was agreed in Geneva, is a delicate balance between a number of competing interests  —  to require sufficient information to be provided to States parties to ensure that their partners comply with their obligations under the Convention, and to maintain the confidentiality of business information that is not subject to disclosure. As noted by one of the negotiators of the Convention, the development of provisions for the verification of the chemical industry was one of the most difficult aspects of the CWC and required lengthy negotiations, starting in 1987 . This difficulty with the verification system of the chemical industry is reflected in several points of the CWC: Article II provides key definitions , Article VI directly sets out the verification regime , the Annex on Chemicals lists certain chemicals falling under the “application of verification measures” , the Annex on Verification contains detailed provisions on declaration and inspection procedures , and the  Confidentiality Annex sets out provisions for the protection of confidential information of the inspected chemical plant   [see Annex 1]. Article II of the CWC is its integral and one of the key parts, since it presents the basic definitions and concepts of the basic and most important terms of the Convention in order to avoid misunderstandings in the terminology used. In this article, one of the main definitions is the concept of chemical weapons. Namely, the clarification that “‎toxic chemicals and their precursors, unless they are intended for purposes not prohibited by this Convention, provided that the types and quantities are consistent with such purposes” can be defined as “criteria for general use”, which means that any toxic chemical, whether listed in the Chemicals Annex or not, is a chemical weapon if it is not produced for purposes not prohibited by this Convention. It can be concluded that the definition of chemical weapons is not based on any material characteristics of specific chemicals, but directly on the intention of the manufacturer, as well as on the types and quantities of chemicals produced. Also, this article presents the definition of “chemical toxic substance” and what chemicals can fall under this definition, in other words, the criteria and properties of substances. It is worth noting that Article II includes an important clarifying index regarding the lists of chemicals included in the CWC, and states that these “‎lists” contain “toxic” chemicals that have been identified for the application of verification measures, that also plays an important role for the chemical industry. Another important paragraph of Article II, which is regarding the chemical industry and its future, is the definition of “peaceful purposes”, includes such concepts as “industrial, agricultural, research, medical, pharmaceutical or other peaceful purposes” . And this article contains another set of definitions specifically related to industry verification: “production”, “processing”, “consumption”. Important part of the Convention for the chemical industry is Article VI “Activities not prohibited by the CWC”, which is directly related to Article II. It is this Article that defines the criteria and framework for the transition from goals of military production to non-military. Experts such as W. Krutch and R. Trapp believe that “Article VI is thus a consequence of the overarching scope of the Convention, on the one hand, and the complexities created by the very nature of chemical weapons, such as the existence of “dual-use” chemicals, on the other” . Article VI establishes the right of States Parties to the CWC “to develop, produce, otherwise acquire, store, transfer and use toxic chemicals and their precursors for purposes not prohibited by the CWC” . The article under review provides a legal basis for “data monitoring and on-site verification” related to data in the Convention, chemicals, facilities, and activities. The types of establishments that are allowed to produce Schedule 1 chemicals are also represented in the CWC. These enterprises are responsible for the preparation of annual declarations on past and prospective activities, and they are subject to international monitoring. The CWC imposes restrictions on the transfer of Schedule 1 chemicals, according to which they can be exported to other member states only for research, medical, pharmaceutical or protective purposes, that is, only for activities not prohibited by the CWC conditions. But Schedule 1 chemicals cannot be exported to states that are not parties to the Convention or returned to other third states. At the same time  for the chemical industry the more important part of the CWC is the Schedules 2 and 3 chemicals. These Schedules include such types of chemicals and their precursors that can be used for peaceful purposes and for goals of military production (“dual-use” chemicals). In this way these chemicals can be a problem for the chemical industry in the field of on-site inspections and other verification.  One of the important conditions regarding the inspection of enterprises is the collection of information necessary to assess the potential risk posed by the facility for the purposes of the CWC. This risk assessment is carried out directly by the Technical Secretariat, which in turn is used as a basis for determining the frequency and intensity of subsequent inspections of chemical facilities. An inspection cannot last more than 96 hours, unless the State Party and the inspection agree on an extension, and no production zone can receive more than two inspections per year . The verification annexes set certain limits on the quantities of Schedules 2 and 3 chemicals, and these annexes refer to the prohibition of transferring and exporting identified chemicals to States not party to the Convention. At the same time, regarding “other chemical production facilities”, Part IX of the Verification Annex requires CWC member states to declare all production sites that have produced more than 200 tonnes of unscheduled “discrete organic chemicals” (OCPFS), or a total of 30 tonnes of unscheduled “discrete organic chemicals” containing the elements fluorine, phosphorus and sulfur. The OCPFS regime was one of the last elements of the CWC verification regime to be agreed at the end of the CWC negotiation process. The presented conditions are fully consistent with the proposals of the chemical industry of June 1991 on free access to all chemical production facilities . As mentioned earlier, the filing of national declarations forms the basis of the CWC verification regime. Thus, the development of declarations was another important issue that required the attention of the governments of the States Parties and the chemical industry. The main purpose of on-site inspections is to verify the accuracy of the statements submitted to the OPCW. The development of the system of declarations  took  very  long time, since during the negotiations many questions arose concerning directly the elements and conditions of the checks that had to be resolved. At the beginning of 1994, a group of experts on inspection procedures was established, combining most of the issues related to the actual conduct of routine inspections. Many of the issues discussed were related to finding a balance between the rights of the inspection team to fulfill the inspection mandate and the rights of the State Party subject to inspection to protect its confidential information. For some delegations, this was a decisive struggle that could significantly affect the effectiveness of the CWC verification regime, developed during the Geneva talks. In addition, an agreement on guidelines for scheduled chemicals in mixtures has been presented, which is important as it affects the number of declared facilities and the reporting of transfers of scheduled chemicals . If countries have different national limits for low concentrations, it may happen that in one country those chemical objects will be declared that are not declared in another state and are in full force. Thus, the chemical industry was particularly interested in the low concentration problem being addressed as soon as possible to ensure a level playing field for the chemical industry and to avoid multinational companies facing different national regulations. Another most important aspect for representatives of the chemical industry in the negotiations on the CWC was the issue of ensuring the confidentiality of corporate information of each of the facilities. By the end of 1993, it had become apparent that perhaps the biggest obstacle to an effective verification and inspection system would be the issue of protecting the corporate information of chemical plants subject to inspection. According to Robert Matthews and Anthony Taubman, the balance between confidentiality and verification efficiency has become one of the most difficult to strike on CWC implementation . The chemical industry's concerns about the confidentiality of their corporate information have sparked many and protracted debates. This issue was repeatedly referred to various expert groups for discussion. The expert group chaired by Anthony Taubman in its report recognized “the need to balance concerns about confidentiality with the requirement of overall transparency and appropriate intrusiveness in verification activities” . This is how the group began developing the OPCW's Privacy Policy project, which set out the basic guiding principles for privacy policies. After numerous meetings and discussions, this program was adopted with some classification for protecting the confidentiality of information of chemical enterprises: With some restrictions from the OPCW  side; Ordinary level of protection by the OPCW; The highest level of protection by the OPCW. The result of this program was the development and creation of an Information Management System (IMS) of OPCW in 1995, in which confidential information was processed and stored . However, the work on the installation of this system was suspended due to disagreements between delegations about the security level of the IMS. Some states insisted on the highest level of security for computer systems, but did not want to provide additional funding for the purchase and implementation of such a system . Members of the OPCW were required to sign a Secrecy Agreement and confidential information was to be available on a need to know basis . The main attention in the developed system was paid to the control and tracking of documents, the creation of an audit trail, so that in the event of misuse of information, it was possible to establish who exactly used it. That required developing a system for registering access, copying and printing documents. The implementation of this program was hampered by the fact that although delegations agreed to store the declarations in electronic form, they did not agree with the possibility of an electronic search for the same declarations . For this reason, it took another two years to convince delegations that the OPCW's IT system and protocols were compliant and that they could be safely scanned and stored electronically. In this way, most of the participating States preferred in this case to use the third, highest level of protection in order to protect their privacy and minimize the negative consequences of possible IT threats . All this was placed under the control of the Technical Secretariat. Given the complex procedures in the Technical Secretariat and the lack of electronic processing, this placed a very heavy burden on the Secretariat staff. The confidentiality issue under consideration has arisen in various expert groups, especially in the negotiation of the equipment and review procedures groups [see Annex 1]. It is important to note that this problem was fully justified by the fears that an inspector, when checking a chemical enterprise, may deliberately collect samples while at the facility for subsequent analysis or sale to the company's competitors, some kind of secret sampling . In addition, there was another concern: contamination from one site could be transferred to another through the inspector's clothing or equipment, which could raise concerns about compliance with health and safety policies. Based on all of the above, the following circle of main problems can be identified that were touched upon at the first stages of the negotiations: The balance between confidentiality of corporate information and verification efficiency. Completeness and relevance of lists of chemicals and their precursors that can be used to create chemical weapons, including «‎dual-use»‎ chemicals. The problem of determining the conditions and mechanisms for the inspection of chemical facilities.   The problem of declaring aggregate national data. Despite some pressure from representatives of the chemical industry, none of these issues were resolved within the framework of the Preparatory Commission, thus these issues came under the jurisdiction of the OPCW. All problems and questions were discussed in one way or another during negotiations and numerous meetings. As a result, States signatories to the Convention had sufficient information and were able to adapt their legal frameworks to fulfill their obligations under the Convention at the national and international levels after its entry into force. During the negotiation process and the preparation of the main text of the Convention, numerous issues of the implementation of the CWC were considered, and a number of technical issues related directly to the activities of chemical enterprises were analyzed, which affected the political component of the Chemical Weapons Convention. The main problems were not only the conditions for the transition from the goals of military production to non-military ones, but one of the most important components of the successful implementation of the CWC was ensuring the security of confidential information of chemical enterprises during both routine and challenge inspections. The definition of specific conditions for the use, production and storage of chemicals affected the future activities of chemical industry facilities, and also influenced the economic sphere of the States parties to the Convention. This active participation of the chemical industry in the discussion and development of the CWC conditions from a technical point of view influenced public opinion, which was initially one of the important reasons why industry representatives became participants in the negotiation process.  Political elites possessed basic technical knowledge that helped to formulate basic definitions for a better understanding of the terms of the Convention. However, it has become an essential part of the work of the chemical industry to categorize the Lists of Chemicals according to their ‎hazard and ‎toxicity levels. This was an important and necessary action for the further development of an effective verification mechanism and system. Thus, at the beginning of the negotiations, rather fundamental issues were raised, such as the development of a verification system and mechanisms, the conditions for inspections, the definition of prohibited chemicals, and the issue of protection of the chemical facilities confidential information. Based on these problems, the ways of further interaction of the chemical industry with the national governments of the CWC member countries at the state level and within the framework of the OPCW's work were built. At the same time, already at the initial stages of negotiations on the Convention, the interaction of the chemical industry with the OPCW yielded results. 1.3. The outcomes of chemical industry involvement in the CWC negotiation Initially interaction between the emerging OPCW and the industry slowed: on the key issues that were mentioned earlier, a final decision was taken and were included in the text of the treaty, so there was no need for further consultations on them. The OPCW was created, but has not yet become a recognized entity and has not begun to actually implement the Convention in practice. States were preparing for ratification and further implementation at the national level and began to amend legislation and national regulations. In addition, an important element in the successful implementation of CWC conditions at the national level was that governments did consult with national industry associations to ensure they had the support of the chemical industry to further lobby for changes in the national legislation. All this was complicated by the fact that there was still no implementation in practice. The situation changed when the CWC was ratified by its 65th participant (Hungary) and entered into force on April 29, 1997 . Since its entry into force, one particular set of problems has arisen as a result of the US delay in enacting its own implementing legislation, resulting in no industry audits in the US during the first year. Thus, the practice adopted by the OPCW Technical Secretariat was based on decisions taken by the Conference of the States Parties in The Hague in 1993 — 1994 and its actual inspections in a number of countries, but not in the case of the United States industry. When the inspections of US companies began, a number of differences of interpretation arose between the United States government and the Technical Secretariat and needed to be resolved on a bilateral basis with the direct involvement of the USA industry. This worked informally, and agreements reached with the national authorities of the United States regarding the chemical industry were embedded in subsequent EU decisions and in the inspection issues of the US Center for Security Policy (CSP).  This was a sign that OPCW needed to continue its dialogue with industry, along with an internal process for resolving the still unresolved issues related to industry review. The First Review Conference was in 2003 to obtain CSP support for direct two-way communication between Technical Secretariat and industry to discuss practical industry verification issues that need to  resolve . This was done on an informal basis using existing relationships with some of the key Technical Secretariat employees who were the chemical industry representatives, held leadership positions and were responsible for implementing the industry audit. Later, this relationship was formalized, which in the following years led to the creation of a kind of permanent advisory body with representatives of the industry. The chemical industry has become actively involved in fulfilling the conditions of the CWC, has begun to pay more attention to cooperation with national authorities for the subsequent strengthening of the CWC regime, which led to a significant increase in the role of the chemical industry in political processes. An important consequence of the involvement of chemical industry representatives in the negotiation process was that all States Parties began to pay more attention to interaction with the chemical industry at the national level with the opportunities to bring ideas and positions of their chemical industry for discussion within the framework of international cooperation. This resulted in the creation of numerous chemical industry associations during and after the negotiation process. These associations began to play an important role and became a kind of instrument, a channel of communication between industry and government. Associations united at the national level to resolve various issues related to industry. At the same time, these associations also entered the international level for the exchange of knowledge and their personnel, which significantly influenced the development of the economy and technological process of states. All kinds of conferences, meetings were convened, seminars and workshops were held, where the issues of industry were discussed in which the authorities could only help in a framework of a rather narrow circle of knowledge in this area. The results of the discussions were fixed in a certain document, which was later submitted for consideration to a representative of the government structure. It was these consequences that gave a big leap in strengthening the position of industry. The Convention touched upon the problems of information security in the chemical industry. Therefore, another consequence of the CWC conditions was the development and implementation of new technologies to protect the information space of the chemical industry. This made it possible to preserve not only the confidential information of chemical enterprises, but also to secure the confidentiality of national programs related to chemical weapons, and to provide information about them only upon request. Despite the fact that the consequences of interaction between the chemical industry and the governments of the States Parties to the Convention are not so many, in terms of their importance they occupy an important place in the subsequent development of the CWC regime. The participation of the chemical industry led not only to the emergence of new elements and factors for further negotiations (in the form of associations of the chemical industry, scientific and technical achievements), but also to changing the role of chemical industry representatives in some political processes. The chemical industry has developed into a permanent consultant with the ability and influence to advance its interests. In this way, based on the analysis of the history of the CWC negotiation process, it can be concluded that the involvement of the chemical industry in the negotiations on the Chemical Weapons Convention was not originally a given. The question of the need for closer contacts with representatives of the chemical sector arose during the first meetings of the Preparatory Commission. Such interaction was beneficial to both parties  —  the chemical industry and the governments of states. The chemical industry has sought primarily to restore its reputation following the incidents involving the use of chemical weapons by Iran against Iraq and attempts to acquire CW by Libya. This left a negative imprint on chemical enterprises, society blamed the industry for all disasters, accidents and incidents where quite dangerous chemicals were used.  As the interaction of representatives of the chemical industry with national authorities deepened, it became clear that the participation of the chemical industry in the development of CWC conditions regarding verification systems, inspection conditions, lists of chemicals and activities prohibited by the Convention is a necessary measure for the further development of the chemical industry and the protection of its corporate interests. from excessive government interference. Based on this, the chemical industry has become very active in the CWC's discussions with their national governments, which has led to the creation of national chemical industry associations such as the British Chemical Industry Association, the Germany chemical industry association VCI, the Japan Chemical Industry Association, and others. These associations interacted with the national authorities like ministries of foreign affairs of their states, which had representatives as part of the delegations to the CWC negotiations. This allowed the development of a kind of multi-channel communication tool between the chemical industry and negotiators, with the help of which the industry began to gradually represent its interests under the Convention. This multi-channel communication mechanism has brought the chemical industry to an international level. This is reflected in the creation of an association of national chemical industry associations  —  the International Council of Chemical Associations (ICCA), which has represented the common interests of the chemical industry of all CWC States Parties in the international arena within the framework of the OPCW's work.  The chemical industry was actively involved in the development of some Articles (II and VI) of the CWC, which dealt directly with the work of chemical enterprises. This was important from the point of view of compliance with technical rules and preservation of confidential information of chemical industries (development of a separate Annex of confidentiality). The chemical industry has made a great contribution to the establishment of the verification system and measures (Annex of the verification). This issue was worked out carefully at the national and international levels, since it was important to note all the features, conditions, types, requirements and technical components of inspections.  At the same time, the governments of the CWC member states also had their own reasons for involving the chemical industry in the negotiation process. The main reason was the desire of governments to develop a verification system that would take into account all specific aspects of the industry and would have effective practical value. However, the political elites understood that only with the support of representatives of the chemical industry  they would  be able to make these changes at the legislative level in their countries. At the same time, there was some difficulty, namely, a thin line between maintaining the confidentiality of corporate information of chemical enterprises and the completeness and reliability of inspections of these enterprises. In this way, the chemical industry paid great attention to the technical component of the implementation of the CWC conditions. This, in turn, led to scientific and technological development and the introduction of new information technologies to ensure the security of the digital environment of the CWC regime. According to the above, the participation of the chemical industry in the negotiation process on the CWC was an important step towards the establishment of a chemical weapons control regime, since the views of the chemical industry on many of the CWC technical components, together with the political core, provide a deeper understanding and analysis of all the strengths and weaknesses of the Chemical Weapons Convention, which accordingly makes it possible to develop such a disarmament and control scheme that will minimize the risks of not only the use of chemical weapons, but also attempts to create and proliferate them. Chapter 2. The CWC in the context of contemporary international relations Since the adoption of the Chemical Weapons Convention, international relations have changed and developed. The development of scientific progress, new scientific achievements, the emergence of new state and non-state actors gave impetus to the formation of new challenges and threats to the implementation of the conditions of the CWC. This chapter examines the role played by the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW) in the realities of the current development of the CWC regime. The main channels and mechanisms of interaction of the chemical industry with the OPCW to increase the efficiency of the CWC are also considered. This chapter summarizes the main challenges and threats that the OPCW and the chemical industry face as they interact with each other and how these issues are addressed. 2.1. The role and place of the OPCW in the CWC The Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW) is an international organization that was established on April 29, 1997. The OPCW was created to “‎implement the provisions of the Convention in order to realize the vision of a world free of chemical weapons and eliminate the threat of their use …” . During the formation of the conditions of the Convention, it was planned to create an executive body that would most effectively exercise control over the implementation of the Convention by all the CWC State Parties, and could authorize and conduct independent examinations and inspections of chemical enterprises. The OPCW has become such an international organization. The original plan was to create a body that would build on the foundations and principles of the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), but with a smaller budget. This was based on the consideration of the participating States that a rather large budget is spent on financing the IAEA, and it would be difficult to finance another such organization, since this would lead to the need to redistribute the entire budget of each participating country, and this, accordingly, would entail other problems related to gaps and budget constraints. Thus, the issue of financing such an organization has become one of the main problems. Since chemical safety and security, although it has been an important component of the entire international security system since the 1980-s, this did not increase the costs of participating States in the same way as it did with IAEA funding . Despite this problem, the OPCW began its existence with the signing and ratification of the Chemical Weapons Convention. The OPCW fulfills two fundamental principles on the basis of which all the work of its bodies is built: firstly, the central place in the multilateral nature of the Convention, and secondly, the equal application of the provisions of the Convention to all State Parties . These principles contribute not only to building a world free of chemical weapons, but also to the stability of international security and the economic development of the participating countries. According to this fact, the activities of the OPCW can be divided into the following areas: Elimination of chemical weapons Preventing reappearance Support for national implementation Promoting the use of chemistry for peace Preparedness and Rapid Response to the Use of Chemical Weapons Victim support. Based on the text of the Convention, the member states must, within 10 years after the entry into force of the CWC, destroy the existing stocks of Category 1 chemical weapons in their territories using special technologies that were also presented in the document. At the same time, the states have the right to extend the period up to an additional 5 years . All activities for the destruction of chemical weapons by the participating countries are carried out under the close supervision of the OPCW. This is a necessary part of the verification system for the successful fulfillment of the conditions of the Convention, primarily for full confirmation of the destruction of chemical weapons, as well as for excluding the fact of diversion of materials for reuse for the production of new chemical weapons. Until the final destruction of chemical weapons, OPCW inspectors carry out routine monitoring of chemical demilitarization activities on an ongoing basis. Inspectors conduct this routine monitoring through a permanent physical presence at the chemical weapons destruction facility and monitoring with on-site instruments such as closed loop cameras and process control screens. Inspectors also have the right to examine the documentation and records that they consider relevant to the performance of their inspection . A number of toxic and chemical substances are widely used around the world, but they can be used for both peaceful and military purposes. Based on the foregoing, States Parties to the Convention should ensure that these chemicals and their precursors are used only for purposes not prohibited by the CWC. The implementation of routine inspections and checks, challenge inspections, and the withdrawal of samples are also part of the mechanism for the timely prevention of the reappearance of chemical weapons.  At the same time, for the effective work of the Convention and the OPCW (as a supervising body) in the territories of the participating countries, it is also necessary to introduce the conditions of the Chemical Weapons Convention directly within the regulatory framework of states. Building on the provisions of Article VII of the CWC, member states need to enact legislation prohibiting individuals and legal entities from engaging in any activity prohibited by the Convention, for example, in the production or use of chemical weapons . They are also required to designate or establish a national authority “which acts as a national focal point for effective communication with the Organization and other States Parties” . Such a process requires a lot of effort and experience, which is sometimes lacking in member countries, and in order to correct this and improve the effectiveness of the Convention, the OPCW Technical Secretariat regularly conducts training courses for staff working with National Authorities in various countries, and the OPCW offers a wide range of programs to support the implementation of the Convention at the national level, such as the Annual Meeting of National Authorities (an annual forum in The Hague for high-level representatives of national authorities and the OPCW to foster interaction and cooperation), an internship program for legal drafters and government officials (special assistance for participating States in drafting national implementing legislation), training course on declarations and inspections (a course to assist national authority personnel and other national stakeholders in preparing declarations and accepting / accompanied and OPCW inspections (in accordance with Article VI). Another important task of the OPCW is to control the use of the achievements of chemistry for peaceful purposes, promote and control the exchange of information, technologies and developments in the chemical field for further use in activities not prohibited by the conditions of the CWC, and aimed at the further development of the State Parties. This task is rather complex and controversial: despite the fact that the Convention encourages such actions, since it contributes to the technical and economic development of the participating countries, at the same time, Article I of the CWC prohibits encouraging and facilitating the production of chemical weapons, and in the Convention also there is a section related to very strict restrictions and a ban on the production of a “dual-use” chemicals that can be used both for peaceful purposes and for the creation of chemical weapons. The challenge for the OPCW is to strike a balance between complying with the ban on chemical weapons, on the one hand, and supporting and facilitating the exchange of knowledge for peaceful purposes and the trade in chemicals to support the development of State Parties, on the other hand. In other words, the Convention requires the OPCW and State Parties to work together to support capacity building, transparency and cooperation. The support programs for the chemical industry enhance the OPCW's ability to counter prohibited activities and enable all countries to benefit from the peaceful uses of chemistry. Based on this fact, it can be concluded that the exchange of information and technology is possible in the conditions of the CWC, but under the strict control of the OPCW. The OPCW has an important position in responding to the use of chemical weapons. The Organization is the same disinterested party when it comes to establishing the fact of the use of chemical weapons. Despite the fact that the initial inspections and investigations are carried out by national authorities, the OPCW is a representative of all participating countries, which means that the inspection team will fairly objectively assess the situation, which makes it possible to make sure that the results of inspections are transparent. Within the OPCW, there are several types of missions: fact-finding missions (FFM), rapid response and assistance missions (RRAM) and technical assistance missions . A fact-finding mission involves examining available information related to allegations of the possible use of chemical weapons, thereby confirming or refuting the use of chemical weapons or suspicions of their production . In the event of emergencies, the Rapid Response and Assistance Mission is required to provide immediate assistance to any CWC State Party that requests it. In particular, this applies to cases of attacks on a particular State Party of the OPCW with the use of chemical weapons . Technical assistance missions include the provision of assistance and advice from the OPCW Technical Secretariat, upon request, to those States Parties that do not need to conduct an investigation or provide a quick response, but would like to benefit from the Secretariat's technical expertise . For example, such experience may be needed to identify the poisonous substances used in the attack. Based on the above, it can be concluded that the OPCW is the fundamental basis for the implementation of the CWC. The organization not only carries out formal monitoring of compliance with the provisions of the Chemical Weapons Convention, but also promotes the implementation and development of relevant legislation at the national level of the CWC States Parties, which meets all the requirements of the Chemical Weapons Convention. The OPCW plays an important role in the further development of the member states in the technological, scientific, economic and political spheres, which does not contradict the conditions of the CWC. On the basis of the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons, there are not only state programs of consultations and interaction, but also scientific and technical programs for development and cooperation, as well as programs involving the interaction between representatives of the OPCW and the chemical industry. This helps to reduce the difference in the development of chemical industry systems, as well as to fill gaps in some issues of internal policy of the participating countries, for example, issues on the distribution of funding, safety policy, etc. The multilevel development of the OPCW States Parties affects a certain range of problems in the implementation of the conditions of the Convention or in the implementation of any program related to activities to strengthen the CWC regime. For example, in 2006, the OPCW concluded a memorandum of mutual assistance with the African Union . In accordance with this memorandum, the OPCW provides support and comprehensive assistance to the African Union countries in the development of the chemical industry, including from the technical and political side, and the Technical Secretariat supported the establishment of an OPCW office in Africa . In this way, African countries have the opportunity to improve the development of their chemical industry and, accordingly, to strengthen their economies associated with chemical production. At the same time, such interaction made it possible to ensure full compliance with the terms of the Convention on the territory of the African Union. Thus, the OPCW is an international organization that monitors the implementation of the provisions of the Chemical Weapons Convention, promoting cooperation and interaction between national authorities of the OPCW States Parties and their chemical industry on the international level (in some cases this interaction can be directly between the OPCW subordinate bodies and national chemical industry associations, for example during training, etc). The OPCW serves as a liaison with civil society, clarifying the terms of the CWC, and directing some of its activities to educate future generations about the dangers of chemical weapons, chemicals and their precursors. The OPCW is an important component of the entire chemical weapons control regime, as it cooperates with the national authorities of the States Parties and the chemical industry of these states through subordinate bodies and committees, exercises direct control over production and facilitates the further transition from goals of military  production to non-military ones. Based on the fact that the OPCW implements numerous workshops, seminars, various political and scientific and technical programs, it is important to note that in the implementation and supervision of such activities, the OPCW is a complex of mechanisms for interaction with representatives of the chemical industry, which include the work of certain subordinate bodies such as the Technical Secretariat, the Executive Board, the Scientific Advisory Board and others. In this way, the OPCW is not only an overseer, but also serves as a kind of bridge between national governments and the chemical industry of all CWC States Parties, as well as those countries that are not members of the CWC and the OPCW. 2.2. Mechanisms and channels of interaction between the OPCW and the chemical industry Starting from the 1980-s  —  1990-s, the chemical industry began to actively participate in the formation of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons. Today, industry is more and more actively involved in the implementation and development of the CWC, since the industrial sector in many participating countries occupies a leading position and affects the economies of states. The channels of communication between the OPCW and the chemical industry take on the form of a rather complex system. Even during the initial negotiations, at the national level special mechanisms were created for the interaction of the governments of countries with their industrial sector. This option includes the expansion of the functions of some national government bodies or the creation of ad hoc committees that are directly responsible for the work and cooperation with representatives of the chemical industry in various fields regarding the CWC. At the same time, a step was also taken on the part of chemical enterprises to deepen and build a more structured work with the national governments of their countries  —  national associations of the chemical industry were created. Examples of such associations are the British Chemical Industry Association (CIA), the German Chemical Industry Association (VCI), the American Chemistry Council (ACC), the Canadian Chemical Manufacturers Association (CCPA), the Japan Chemical Industry Association (JCIA), and others. These associations include representatives from various chemical industries throughout the country. Each association is responsible for cooperating with the government bodies of their states, uniting and representing the interests of all chemical enterprises within their state. In addition to solving current issues and problems, the associations contribute to the further development of work within the framework of the fulfillment of the conditions of the CWC at the national level (according to the obligations of the CWC Article VI). The chemical industry associations of the member states, together with national authorities, organize and supervise all kinds of seminars, workshops, training aimed at exchanging information, working out controversial issues, as well as modernizing and strengthening the CWC regime within the state. At the same time, national authorities are responsible for funding national programs in relation to the chemical industry in ensuring control over the timely implementation of all safety requirements and measures and the proper condition of equipment at chemical plants. It is also worth noting that the national authorities are the first instance where representatives of the state's chemical industry turn to, especially when it comes to emergencies. For example, in the Russian Federation, the Ministry of Industry and Trade monitors interaction with the chemical industry, supervision and inspection . The Ministry of Industry and Trade is responsible not only for the state and development of the chemical industry on the territory of Russia, but is also a link in the conduct of inspections at chemical enterprises of the Russian Federation by the OPCW . The next level of interaction is international. It, in turn, includes several channels of communication between the chemical industry and the governments of the CWC States Parties. To reach the international level, national chemical industry associations need to unite and develop a system of common goals and objectives. This activity is coordinated by the International Council of Chemical Associations (ICCA) . This organization is virtual and brings together national and regional associations of the chemical industry. ICCA oversees the work of individual members or parts of associations and member companies of these associations . The International Council of Chemical Associations sets itself not only to help chemical enterprises with their finances, but is also responsible for coordination in international global problems, including international security. Thus, cooperation between the OPCW and the ICCA is one of the important aspects of the implementation of the CWC and compliance with its regime. The Council participates in regional seminars organized by the OPCW, is represented on the Advisory Board on Education and Outreach, the Scientific Advisory Board and addresses the participating states at the annual Conference of States Parties . “Improving collaboration with the OPCW offers the ICCA a unique opportunity to engage in structured dialogue with the organization and reach out to industry and governments in emerging economies to further advance responsible care,” said René Van Sloten, CEFIC's Executive Director of Industrial Policy, in an interview. “This opportunity aligns well with ICCA's goal of spreading the ethics of responsible care in specific regions and countries.” In addition, ICCA participates in events related to chemical safety, environmental chemistry and the chlorine industry. There is also an OPCW Partnership Program that provides technical assistance to chemical engineers from developing countries and provides an opportunity to learn about current practices in chemical companies . In other words, the OPCW and ICCA have not only practical interaction, but also legally enshrined agreements on programs and areas of cooperation and development . At the OPCW level, the Technical Secretariat operates as a channel of communication with representatives of the chemical industry, including individual chemical enterprises, national chemical industry associations and the International Council of Chemical Associations. The functions of the Technical Secretariat are quite extensive and include: assistance to States Parties in the development of programs for protection against chemical weapons; support and development of international cooperation in the field of chemistry for peaceful purposes; providing technical assistance to States Parties, including the chemical industry, to ensure full implementation of the Chemical Weapons Convention; and disseminating information about the CWC through workshops and public relations events . This activity is aimed at building trust between the governments of the participating states and the chemical industry, discussing the current challenges and threats faced by representatives of the chemical industry. It is important to note that the Technical Secretariat pays great attention to clarifying the conditions of the CWC for representatives of the chemical industry. This is primarily related to understanding the Schedules of prohibited chemicals and their precursors, activities prohibited and not prohibited by the terms of the Convention. At the same time, numerous programs are being implemented aimed at the exchange of scientific and technical personnel for advanced training, and the exchange of knowledge and technical advances that can increase production efficiency. The Technical Secretariat is considering a proposal or any other document related to the CWC activities. If the proposal or request relates to budget allocation, clarification of issues related to the Convention, as well as challenge inspections and investigations of the alleged use of CW, the Technical Secretariat forwards this document to the Executive Council. If the promotion of international cooperation for peaceful purposes in the field of chemical activities  and an overview of scientific and technological developments that may affect the Convention are being considered,  in that case these issues are referred directly to the Conference of the States Parties for further discussion . Among other things, the interaction of the chemical industry with representatives of the OPCW takes place through the work of non-governmental organizations such as the Pugwash CBW research group and the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI). Both organizations are conducting extensive detailed research related not only to the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons, but also to the possibilities of further cooperation between the governments of the States Parties and representatives of the chemical industry to increase the effectiveness of the implementation of the CWC conditions. Every year, SIPRI and Pugwash hold all kinds of seminars and workshops that cover both national authorities circles and the chemical industry . These meetings provide an opportunity to consider issues of current cooperation, discuss gaps in the verification system and implementation of the CWC conditions. These organizations help the chemical industry to clearly structure and address emerging problems and come out with them on the international arena for the CWC member countries, as representatives of SIPRI and Pugwash quite often participate in meetings, seminars, and some government conferences organized by the OPCW.
