Today, the chemical industry is quite active in interacting with national authorities and the OPCW. The channels of communication are diverse, ranging from domestic chemical industry associations to the International Committee of Chemical Associations and the involvement of government bodies and nongovernmental organizations. This makes it possible to cover a wide range of issues that require more detailed and careful consideration. Based on the foregoing, the channel of interaction between the chemical industry and the OPCW can be divided into the following groups: National level National chemical associations National authorities 2) International level International Council of Chemical Associations OPCW subordinate bodies (Technical Secretariat, Executive Council, Scientific Advisory Board) National authorities Non-governmental organizations (Pugwash, SIPRI) From the presented classification, it is important to highlight the fact that national authorities are not only a channel of communication at both levels of interaction between the chemical industry and the OPCW. It is the national authorities who decide to join the Convention or not, to join the partnership programs that the OPCW implements, or not. The chemical industry of any member state cannot directly interact with the OPCW, and the OPCW, in turn, cannot direct decisions, decrees, requests, etc., to specific chemical industry representatives. All this activity is carried out through the national authorities. In other words, it is the national authorities that are the starting point for interaction between the chemical industry and the OPCW. Through various seminars, workshops, meetings and training, the OPCW Technical Secretariat has the opportunity to clarify the meaning of the provisions of the Convention for all parties and refer certain issues for further consideration by the OPCW Executive Council and the Conference of the States Parties. Thus, through the activities of the Technical Secretariat, the Executive Council, the Conference of the States Parties and other subsidiary bodies, like the Confidentiality Committee and the Scientific Advisory Board, the OPCW collaborates with national authorities and further with representatives of the chemical industry on issues arising during the implementation of the terms of the Convention, as well as response to emerging threats (accidents / disasters / terrorism at chemical plants) and rapid response to them. 2.3. Current challenges and threats to the CWC in the framework of the interaction of the chemical industry with the OPCW Despite the fact that the interaction between the chemical industry and the OPCW includes various forms and types and is quite actively developing, there are issues that require more careful and detailed consideration. Since the adoption of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons, the threats and challenges faced by the participants in the Conference on the CWC have changed and continue to evolve. Based on this fact, the following groups of threats and challenges can be distinguished: International Chemical terrorism Chemical safety and security  Funding issues  Slowing down decision-making process on current issues During the negotiations in 1990-s, many of the above problems were touched upon, but scientific progress does not stand still, technologies are developing, new state and non-state actors appear, which creates conditions for the emergence and modernization of new threats to the CWC regime. The problem of international terrorism is global and fundamental in matters of ensuring international security. This problem in the chemical sphere is associated with several gaps in the terms of the Chemical Weapons Convention, which in one way or another influence the emergence of the problem of terrorism: the presence of “‎dual-use”‎ chemicals, the imperfection of the export control system, the presence of Black Markets and illegal trading. Under the terms of the CWC, “dual-use”‎ chemicals  —  chemicals that can be used for both military production and civilian production   —  are included in Schedule 3 of the CWC banned chemicals and their precursors . The issue of control over Schedule 3 chemicals is still a problem for the national authorities of the CWC States Parties, since it is rather difficult to track for what purposes these chemicals will later be used. For example, phosgene, which was used as a weapon during World War I and is now widely used in plastics, pesticides, and other products; trimethylphosphite is a precursor for the production of insecticides and flame retardants, which is not prohibited, but can be used as a precursor for nerve agents. Despite the fact that chemical production is under the control of national governments, there is an assertion that during the inspection of a chemical facility, documentation and concealment of facts are possible, which subsequently leads to the transfer of chemicals to third parties by selling them on the Black Markets. The Black Markets' activities are almost impossible to trace either by national authorities or by the international community. The illegal sale of chemicals, precursors, technologies, equipment and even confidential information takes place through the Black Markets. In this case, the ‎seller‎ for the sale of goods receives a certain reward, which can also be used to purchase other prohibited goods.  “‎Dual-use”‎ chemicals and technical equipment are exported in rather large quantities by various countries, which accordingly does not guarantee their further peaceful use. Against this backdrop, it is important to note the activities of the Australian Group, which was formed in 1985 with the aim of using an export licensing system to ensure tighter export controls for certain types of chemicals, biological agents, and chemical and biological technologies and equipment for dual use, which could be used for the production and distribution of chemical and biological weapons . Export control measures implemented by the Australian Group's member states contribute to the creation of a favorable and safe environment for the legal trade in chemicals and biologicals. Thus, the activities of the Australian Group are not a barrier to the work of the OPCW, rather, on the contrary, they expand the range of cooperation measures with the chemical industry in the export control framework, which contributes to wider coverage to ensure the CWC regime. Returning to the problem of international chemical terrorism, to a greater extent it is the Black Markets that are among the first suppliers of chemical weapons, banned chemicals and technologies for terrorist organizations and groups. The problem of international terrorism is global and fundamental in matters of ensuring international security. The most striking example of the use of chemical weapons and the presence of chemical terrorism‎ is Syria. According to various sources, since 2013, the international community has received many complaints about the use of chemical weapons in Syrian territories. According to some sources, chemical attacks were carried out by terrorist organizations such as the Islamic State (banned in Russia), Hayat Tahrir al-Sham (banned in Russia) . At the same time, there is another opinion that the chemical attacks were sanctioned by the government of Bashar al-Assad, but the government itself denies this . Despite all the speculation, the OPCW carried out an inspection and sampling, which subsequently confirmed the existence of the fact of the use of chemical weapons in Syria (chlorine as a nerve gas) . The fight against chemical terrorism is a combination of the above measures, namely, control over the “dual-use”‎ chemicals at the national and international levels, strict export controls, and assistance to the work of special services in the fight against Black Markets and illegal trading. Among other things, there is the problem of chemical safety and security management. This issue includes, first of all, the adoption and implementation of standards and requirements for the safety of chemical plants. In other words, each chemical enterprise, depending on the level of hazard‎ of its production, must develop a certain list of measures and standards that must be observed by workers in chemical production, as well as standards for the state of equipment and technologies. Life safety is one of the main tasks of the security service of chemical and petrochemical enterprises. At the same time, it is worth noting that violation of accepted standards at chemical enterprises leads not only to risks for the lives of employees of these enterprises but can also lead to serious and catastrophic consequences and accidents that jeopardize environmental safety and the lives of the entire population of the country. It is important to note that the conditions of the CWC do not imply response measures to accidents and emergencies occurring through the fault of the employee or equipment malfunction. Therefore, within the OPCW, the Conference of State Parties has announced a program to promote chemical safety and security by providing tools and knowledge to reduce the risks associated with chemical accidents and the potential abuse of toxic chemicals, including the threat of terrorism. This program aims to ensure that chemicals are used only for peaceful purposes throughout their entire life cycle . The first two presented problems are somehow connected with the problem of uneven distribution of funding. Due to the uneven distribution of funds by the governments of states, failures and omissions appear in one area or another, for example, in the field of ensuring chemical safety and security. The budget is an important component for the full and effective functioning of chemical industry enterprises. If national authorities decide to reduce the share of funding in the field of chemical safety and security, then as a negative consequence of that  step can be the failure of machinery and equipment, savings on the means of protecting workers, and so on. And this can lead to an industrial emergency or a man-made accident.  The 2001 AZF blast is a prime example of how unequal resource allocation and safety savings can lead to massive disaster . The accident claimed the lives of 30 people, injured thousands, and the explosion destroyed many houses near the plant. However, the main thing in this incident is that, although the official cause of the explosion has not been established, according to preliminary data from the investigation, the storage conditions of ammonium nitrate and other chemicals did not comply with safety regulations . As experience shows, if in an international organization there are states with different levels and rates of development, including those with multilevel and multidirectional funding, then some programs of this organization will have problems in implementation. As an example, again, it can be the support programs from the OPCW in the states of the African Union . This is due to the fact that the AU states have problems with budget and financing. National governments are forced to make a more rigid decision on the distribution of the budget, which, accordingly, due to the existing difficulties in Africa, leads to omissions in the programs for the implementation of the conditions of the CWC . At the same time, in the past few years, in the interaction of the OPCW and the chemical industry, there has been a shift in emphasis from solving current issues to political differences between the States Parties. The problem is that there is the transition from the current agenda, for example, programs to support the chemical industry of developing countries, inspections, etc., to the discussion of issues not related to the problems of the chemical industry such as the presence of chemical weapons in Syria . The States Parties are going into a long controversy over the disagreements between the governments of the parties to the Syrian conflict, including the confrontation between Russia and the Western bloc. Due to such actions of the participating states, the current issues of ensuring the CWC regime and interaction with the chemical industry to strengthen it are relegated to the background and there is no time left to discuss them in full. It is important to note that this began to happen not only during the Conferences of the States Parties but also at the sites of joint work of the national authorities with representatives of the chemical industry. In this way, the presented problems took place in the 1990-s, but their manifestation was not so significant. The development of science and technology, the emergence of new state and non-state actors contributed to the development and expansion of these problems, which led to an increase in risks to international security. Each of the listed problems affects in its own way the provision of the CWC regime and its effectiveness. At the same time, the development of interactions between the OPCW and the chemical industry has opened up new previously unavailable opportunities that can help address the current political challenges facing the OPCW and the chemical industry. Today, the OPCW and national authorities, with the active participation of the chemical industry, face an important and difficult task  —  to find ways to control the illegal trade and illicit production of prohibited substances, including chemical weapons, to stop their transfer to third parties for military use and purposes that undermine the entire security system, including in the political, economic and socio-cultural spheres. The interaction of the OPCW with representatives of industry at the present stage of development of international relations is reduced not only to strict compliance with the provisions of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons but also to the expansion of these functions for more effective compliance with the CWC regime and ensuring international security. Like any other, the chemical security system requires an executive body that will monitor the activities of the States Parties to the Convention, as well as the adaptability of the CWC conditions to the changing realities of international relations. Despite some funding difficulties, the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons was established. The OPCW's activities cover various spheres of the life of the world community. At the same time, the OPCW not only monitors the fulfillment of the conditions of the CWC but also serves as a common platform for cooperation between the national authorities of the OPCW member states with representatives of the chemical industry in the international arena. The OPCW is an important integral part of the chemical weapons control regime, since it not only interacts with the chemical industry and the national authorities of the participating countries, and controls the transition from military production targets to non-military ones, but also contributes to the adaptation and development of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons in Modern realities of international relations, which makes it possible to work on strengthening the CWC regime. Based on the deepening interaction between the chemical industry and the OPCW, a clear structure of channels and communication mechanisms in their interaction began to form. This structure includes two main levels  —  national and international. At the same time, national authorities are the primary channel of communication between the chemical industry and the OPCW, which creates a three-way channel between the OPCW, the chemical industry and national authorities. Also, interaction takes place through the involvement of national associations of the chemical industry, their unification into the International Council of Chemical Associations, with the participation of subordinate bodies of the OPCW (Technical Secretariat, Executive Council, Scientific Advisory Board). It is important to note that, on a par with the presented communication channels, there is another option for interaction  —  through Non-Governmental Organizations such as SIPRI and Pugwash. The very interaction with representatives of the chemical industry takes place through their participation in various governmental and non-governmental meetings, seminars, workshops and programs, for example, for the exchange of professional personnel, or in the framework of the exchange of information on scientific and technical achievements that can be used in chemical production. Today, interaction between the chemical industry and the OPCW is reduced not only to verifying compliance with the CWC conditions, but also to jointly combating challenges and threats that destabilize chemical security.  The chemical industry and the OPCW also face political challenges in interacting with each other. These problems include both the classic problems of international chemical terrorism and the problems of chemical safety and security management, as well as the problems of financing, uneven distribution of the budget and one of the most pressing problems of modern international relations  —  the participating states devote more time and attention to disputes and disagreements between itself, and the solution of current issues is relegated to the background, which ultimately leads to an incomplete and incomplete consideration of these issues (various support programs for developing countries, strengthening chemical safety measures in industries, etc.).  Due to the shifting interests of the CWC member states, little attention is paid to the state of technical equipment at production facilities, and little budget is allocated for updating the protection systems of chemical enterprises. These omissions ultimately lead to man-made disasters and accidents at chemical plants. At the same time, when the issue concerns meetings on personnel exchange programs, training, etc., which most often take place with the participation of representatives of national authorities and the chemical industry, these meetings may eventually end with a discussion of disagreements over, for example, the Syrian issue. Thus, despite all attempts to strengthen the regime of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons by the States Parties, there are a number of problems that arise, including in the interaction of the OPCW and the chemical industry. Today, the main task facing the OPCW, including the chemical industry, is to find the fine line that will allow the OPCW to distance interactions with the chemical industry from the political disagreements of the participating countries, preventing the use of cooperation platforms with the chemical industry as elsewhere. one arena for political confrontation. Otherwise, all this can lead to a weakening of the CWC regime, as well as to negative consequences for the chemical industry like terrorist attacks on chemical enterprises, accidents, disasters that will lead to the destruction of enterprises, a threat to life, to large financial losses, bankruptcy. Consequently, representatives of the chemical industry, as well as national governments, have an interest in ensuring a CWC regime that will allow them to function and develop normally. Chapter 3. The future of the CWC in the context of the development of the chemical industry The current state of the Chemical Weapons Convention is rather uncertain. In the international arena, there is an increasing split among states, the level of mistrust is growing. This can be justified by the transition to multipolarity in international relations. This transition period negatively affects the CWC regime, as implementation of the Convention's conditions is weakening, and the States Parties are increasingly focusing on differences among themselves. Thus, this chapter analyzes the completeness and the level of sufficiency of the OPCW activities in the modern conditions of the development of international relations. It also addresses the issues that were raised at the stages of the formation of the Convention’s conditions, but were not thoroughly considered, which led to an analysis of how the CWC can be implemented in the future and what needs to be done to strengthen the regime of the Convention. Finally, this chapter presents how changes and developments in the chemical industry affect the functioning of the CWC and how the chemical industry can affect the CWC regime and the entire system of international chemical security. 3.1. Analysis of the OPCW activities’ completeness and sufficiency in modern realities The Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW) occupies one of the leading positions in ensuring the regime of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (CWC). The OPCW not only fulfills the formal functions of monitoring the implementation of the CWC conditions, but also contributes to any development of the Convention, including adaptation of the Convention in the changing realities of international relations, so that the chemical security system remains stable, strengthens regardless of the emergence of new threats or challenges and developed for the benefit of the entire world community. The system for ensuring chemical security is multistage. The OPCW is faced with the task of bringing together all the structures responsible for ensuring security both at the national level of the CWC member states and at the international level. At the same time, the Organization includes work in various spheres of human life: economy, trade, military security, social, as well as scientific, technical and educational spheres. All this places a great responsibility on the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons as the main coordination and control mechanism of the CWC. Despite the fact that initially it was planned to create such an executive international body, which would include as many states as possible and whose goals and objectives they adhere to, the OPCW over time faced the main problem of all international organizations that were created on the basis of international agreements and treaties, which necessity was dictated by the certain situations. This problem lies in the fact that the original purpose of the creation of the Organization can exhaust or change over time, which means that the Organization is faced with a choice: either to cease to exist, or to carry out reforms and reorganization in order to adapt to current changes. Based on the official data presented on the official website of the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons, about 98% of chemicals and chemical weapons have been destroyed since the entry into force of the Convention and the establishment of the OPCW . Based on this fact, the OPCW cannot be disbanded, but it needs to go through a certain adaptation period. At the same time, there is another problem  —  the OPCW, like other organizations, is subject to polarization. In other words, the OPCW, in one way or another, is subject to a clash of political interests and disagreements between the CWC States Parties. In this regard, there is a polarization of the policy pursued within the OPCW, which can be observed in all sorts of controversial issues regarding the Syrian chemical file, as well as situations with the poisoning of Skripals and Alexei Navalny . As the Permanent Representative of the Russian Federation to the OPCW, Alexander Vasilyevich Shulgin, noted: “The Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons continues to be in a protracted crisis. And first of all, it is a crisis of trust, a lack of readiness to hear each other and be heard, to be partners, not adversaries on the Hague site” . The OPCW representatives believe that today the Organization is a pillar of compliance with the regime of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons and does not experience any crisis but rather meets speculation and provocations regarding the policy pursued by the OPCW on the part of those who disagree, for example, Russia . Based on the official data provided by the numerous reports of the working groups of the Organization, at present one can observe a rather large increase in the success of the member countries in fulfilling their obligations under the CWC. However, there is a well-founded fear about the impartiality of the inspections, which can be seen from the examples of investigations into the establishment of the facts of the use of chemical weapons in the Syrian Duma and other territories of Syria. The problem of mistrust within the OPCW is justified by the general political and economic disagreements between states in the international arena outside the Organization, which negatively affects the integrity of the OPCW and the stability of the regime of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons. Polarization and division within the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons have a negative impact on the effectiveness of the fulfillment of the conditions of the CWC, as well as on the fight against common challenges and threats, which are even more aggravated by the coalition differences. In other words, the OPCW member states are fixated on confrontation among themselves and are trying to defend their innocence, while the same threat of chemical terrorism is gaining strength and increasing. The adoption of decisions and agreements on current issues is relegated to the background, often not being fully considered in one meeting, meeting or meeting, since, for example, the discussion of issues of national financing of chemical enterprises can turn into disputes and disagreements between States Parties that there are risks of financing terrorist organizations by national authorities. Such delays and accusations can lead to irreparable consequences and global catastrophe. Despite the attempts of Western states, primarily the United States and Great Britain, to idealize the sufficiency of the OPCW activities, the crisis within the Organization goes beyond its framework. This is evidenced by the fact that in 2018, at a special session of the Conference of the States Parties, a unilateral decision was made to expand the functions of the OPCW and assign the Organization the ability to name the culprits of chemical attacks and the use of banned chemicals and their precursors . Such actions have caused a great storm of indignation, as this decision strikes at the influence of the UN Security Council (UNSC). It was the UN Security Council that initially performed the function of determining the guilty. Now the actions of the OPCW have caused a large-scale resonance both in the international political arena and within scientific and research circles, as there is an overlap and duplication of the functions of two large international organizations. This decision led to a conflict of interest between the organizations, and also further spurred the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons to split. It is important to note that in the presented situation there are attempts to limit the veto of Russia in the UN Security Council through the expansion of the functions of the OPCW, which again leads to controversial incidents in the investigation of the use of chemical poisonous substances in the territories of the Syrian state . Chemical industry officials are also concerned about the tense political situation within the Organization. Despite the fact that the OPCW is not the only liaison body between government and industry at the international level, the representatives of the chemical industry rely to a greater extent on the representatives of the OPCW. Mechanisms for cooperation between the OPCW and chemical enterprises already have a well-formed structure and function quite effectively. From the point of view of the chemical industry, the dissolution of the OPCW will be disadvantageous for the chemical industry of each CWC member state. However, representatives of the chemical industry do not exclude the existence of a crisis within the OPCW, which also affects the chemical industry . The disagreement and polarization of the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons policy puts the system of inspection and verification at chemical facilities in a rather difficult position. Due to complicated political relations between certain states, the chemical industry is under pressure primarily during inspections and suspicions, sometimes not clearly justified, of violation of the terms of the Chemical Weapons Convention. It follows from this that the chemical industry is trying to adhere to a fairly neutral position, as long as disagreements and disputes in the government echelons of the OPCW member countries do not affect the production and operation of chemical enterprises . At the same time, it is worth noting that, speaking about the sufficiency of the OPCW, the chemical industry is of the opinion that the Organization is  successfully enough performing its functions, which, first of all, is reflected in the almost complete destruction of chemical weapons, in providing technical assistance and support to many chemical enterprises in developing countries, the development of partnerships between representatives of the chemical industry of different countries, as well as in assisting in the development and implementation of scientific and technological achievements to improve the efficiency of production facilities . Institutional crisis, growing mistrust among the members of the OPCW and increasing fragmentation of the entire Organization lead to problems that, together, could undermine the existence of the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons in the future. The polarization of the OPCW inspection policies, methods and types of verification increases confrontation between the main participants in the CWC  —  the United States, Russia, Great Britain, France, Germany and others, which in turn leads to politicization  —  to give a political coloring, to strengthen the connection with politics   —  of all activities of the Organization, including interaction with the chemical industry. This can become a catalyst for stagnation and postponement of decision-making on emerging issues, including the problem of combating common threats and challenges, such as chemical terrorism or chamical safety and security management.  Slowing down the decision-making process on current issues can lead to a decrease in the effectiveness of the implementation of OPCW programs, thereby increasing the risk of chemical weapons production, their illegal proliferation through clandestine enterprises and black markets. This, in turn, leads to a decrease in the level of sufficiency of the Organization, and calls into question the ability of the OPCW to ensure overall control over compliance with the conditions of the CWC. Making a conclusion, it can be argued that the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons has a fairly high level of sufficiency within the CWC regime, which is proved by the fact that almost 98% of chemical weapons have been destroyed. The OPCW, together with the national authorities of the participating countries, together with representatives of the chemical industry, are successfully implementing many support and cooperation programs, as well as programs for the development and implementation of new technologies, training, and so on. The Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons also conscientiously fulfills its obligations under the Convention, working not for quantity, but for the quality of compliance with the conditions of the CWC. However, changes in the international environment, primarily in relations between states, namely, the strengthening of disagreements and contradictions between leading states, such as the United States, Russia and some countries of the European Union, the emergence of new political players in the person of unrecognized states, state and non-state actors, NGOs, the modernization of old and the emergence of new threats to the entire system of international security have become destabilizing factors for the work of the OPCW and ensuring the stability of the CWC regime. Modern trends in the development of international relations and the transition to multipolarity  —  a system of international relations where several states or groups of states act as the main players in the international arena  —  have led the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons to an adaptation crisis. The situation with the UN Security Council and the unilateral expansion of the functions of the OPCW only exacerbated the transition period of the Organization. It was this incident that contributed to the expression for the first time since 1997 of the idea of ​​leaving the OPCW, which is nonsense in the history of the Organization . Despite the fact that this idea was mentioned in passing and it was noted that this step is radical, the very fact of the idea of ​​leaving the Organization takes place. At the same time, the positions of the Russian state are supported by other countries such as China, India, Iran, Syria and South Africa . This division is very important when analyzing the politicization and polarization of the OPCW policies. The aforementioned block of States Parties is of the opinion that Western states, primarily the United Kingdom and the United States, through the OPCW are trying to push their positions against the government of Bashar al-Assad in Syria and anti-Russian rhetoric, including limiting the veto and influence of Russia in the UN Security Council, for which and a decision was taken to expand the functions of the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons . There is a question about the integrity and transparency of inspections carried out at chemical plants, as well as the impartiality of the reports issued by the inspection teams, for example, this may be related to the seizure and forgery of seized samples of chemicals. Based on the definition of sufficiency, which includes the quality of being good enough , it can be concluded that today the OPCW is a sufficient international organization to ensure the regime of the CWC, which is proved by statistical indicators and the effectiveness of the programs carried out. However, the existing political problems, which also arise in the interaction of the OPCW with the chemical industry, are challenges and threats to ensure the CWC regime. A decrease in the OPCW sufficiency level could lead to negative consequences for representatives of the chemical industry. Due to the fact that for the chemical industry the OPCW is the main international platform where representatives of the chemical industry can defend their interests through their national authorities, the decline of the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons and complete politicization its activities, including scientific and technical platforms, can lead to that the chemical industry will go unheard. In turn, the political problems that were listed earlier will lead to the decline of the chemical enterprises themselves, which, accordingly, will negatively affect the economies of the participating states. 3.2. Securing the CWC Regime in the Future The Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons became not only the basic document in the field of chemical safety, after the Geneva Protocol of 1925, but also gave rise to a full and comprehensive arms control regime in the chemical sphere. The CWC regime covers several areas, first of all, it is direct disarmament and control over existing chemicals used to create new chemical weapons. Today, the Chemical Weapons Convention regime accounts for about 98% of destroyed chemical weapons and chemical materials, and includes a structured verification system at both the national and international levels. During the initial negotiations and the preparation of the final text of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons, many problems were raised, however, some of them, basic ones, formed the basis of the CWC, thereby defining the Convention as a series of measures aimed at solving these problems  —  complete disarmament, destruction of accumulated stocks of chemical weapons, chemicals and their precursors, the transition of chemical production from military production to non-military production, as well as prevention of further production of chemical weapons and their transfer to third parties. Today, these problems are relevant in the context of the modern development of the CWC regime. This allows the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons and its regime to maintain a certain universality, regardless of the stage of development of the entire system of international security and the system of international relations. Building on the terms of the CWC, universality in the context of chemical safety is at the heart of achieving a comprehensive verifiable prohibition of chemical weapons. It anchors the international norm prohibiting chemical weapons, strengthens the Convention as a confidence-building measure, prevents proliferation, and helps deny non-state actors access to chemical weapons . Despite the fact that the destruction of all chemical stocks is nearing completion, as mentioned earlier, the threat of chemical terrorism remains relevant to this day. Universality is critical to preventing the re-emergence of these weapons and blocking access to them for non-state actors, since the OPCW actions cover a wide range of spheres of life of the States Parties. It is also important to note the fact that the policy of universality of the Convention presupposes the interaction not only of the member states between each other, but also the involvement of states that are not part of the CWC. This is necessary in order to demonstrate the values ​​of the Convention and its contribution to regional and international peace and security. These actions are governed by the 2003 Chemical Weapons Convention Universality Action Plan . On the basis of the presented Plan, it is planned to improve interaction not only between states but also with representatives of the chemical industry, including chemical industry of states that are not members of the CWC, with the help of various events and activities of the Executive Council and the Technical Secretariat . Despite the fact that the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons is taking many steps to ensure the universality of the CWC regime, the tasks that were put forward at the initial negotiations remain either not resolved or not fully resolved.  It follows from this that from the moment of the initial negotiations to the present day, the problems of international chemical terrorism, chemical safety and security management, as well as the problem of financing, remain on the agenda. In the modern realities of the development of international relations, these problems are aggravated by the complex political situation in the world. Disagreements between the leading States Parties negatively affect the interactions of the chemical industry with the OPCW and national authorities. Considering the provision of the CWC regime within the framework of the problem of international chemical terrorism, the following factors can be identified that hinder the successful functioning of the Convention: - Availability of “dual-use” chemicals - Export control problem  Due to the fact that “dual-use” chemicals, according to the definition, can be used for purposes not prohibited by the terms of the Convention, some unscrupulous chemical companies and enterprises may use loopholes in the legislation of their states and, accordingly, in the CWC to get more benefits. The Convention provides for the inclusion of known “dual-use” chemicals in the CWC Schedule 3  but the terms of the Convention do not include any special methods and restrictions on the use of these chemicals, other than the fact that there is a register of registered chemical enterprises specializing in the use of “dual-use” chemicals, whose activities are twice monitored by OPCW inspection teams to prevent any violations of the CWC conditions . However, such measures remain ineffective and can be easily circumvented both nationally and internationally through illegal operations and transport of these substances for further illicit use and transfer to third parties such as terrorist organizations. The problem of “dual-use” chemicals affects the implementation of export controls under the CWC regime. To fill the gaps in the presented issue, it is necessary to develop a more coordinated control over the use of “dual-use” chemicals and their precursors within chemical enterprises themselves. Based on the fact that the violation of the provisions of the Convention on this issue occurs due to the desire of some enterprises to increase their income, it is possible that there is also a need to think over a system of incentives for the proper implementation of national and international legislation to comply with the provisions of the Chemical Weapons Convention. It is necessary to intrigue and interest chemical enterprises in fulfilling the conditions of the CWC but not through the “stick” but through the “carrot”, since otherwise the regularity of the usual psychological factor is activated  —  the more pressure and prohibition, the greater the desire to violate this prohibition. Another important section of the securing of the regime of the Chemical Weapons Convention is ensuring the information security of the chemical industry. As noted earlier, the problem of maintaining confidentiality and the implementation of information security at chemical enterprises [see Annex 1] is one of the most urgent today. Initially, during the initial negotiations, the emphasis was on the translation of paper documentation into digital format. However, the fears of some representatives of the chemical industry and their governments were not in vain. The Technical Secretariat was tasked with developing a common system that would securely exchange and store confidential information regarding chemical production. Based on the fact that in modern international relations there is massive  digitalization  and computerization, the field of information security is increasingly coming to the fore. On the one hand, the introduction of new information technologies can significantly strengthen the CWC regime, on the other, the IT sphere, in view of its rapid development, is also the most vulnerable part of ensuring the regime of the Convention on the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons. In the early negotiations, the participating states could not predict how much computers would affect the enforcement of the Convention. It is important to note that this development of information technologies brought modernization and the emergence of new threats  —  terrorist organizations began to resort more to attacks on the information systems of states, thereby undermining life within the state  —  cyber terrorism. The transfer of confidential information has also become possible without leaving the home. It is quite easy for hackers, if the information security system is flawed or not strong enough from hacking, to penetrate the system of a chemical enterprise and steal confidential information in order to sell it to competitors of this enterprise or use it for other purposes, for example, international terrorism. To solve this problem, it is necessary to convey to all CWC participants as accurately as possible the importance of improving and monitoring the quality of the information security system of chemical industry enterprises: timely software updates, protection against hacking, backup servers and cloud storage, a multi-level system of information security and secrecy, and much more. Since among some CWC States Parties the information sphere lags behind the development of the same USA, China, Russia, the OPCW needs to think over a program of assistance in updating and developing information technologies of these states. It is worth noting that even at the initial negotiations, the issue was raised about the different levels of development of IT communications in the participating countries . And, despite the fact that a common base was created, the question of the internal development of the information structures of these states remained open. The exchange of personnel and knowledge makes it possible to identify the vulnerabilities of these states, however, there is still no more active program for updating and reforming information systems, which can be justified only by two reasons  —  the budget and the direct complexity of communication of all information structures of the entire state. It is impossible to increase the level of development of information systems only chemical enterprises. Based on this, in order to strengthen the CWC regime in the future, the problem of ensuring information security requires more attention from the participating countries with the direct participation of representatives of the chemical industry. In turn, there is another important aspect that was touched upon in the initial negotiations but the measures to resolve this issue in the CWC were quite general in nature. It is a chemical safety and security management problem. At the time of the adoption of the Chemical Weapons Convention, the importance of this issue was noted only for situations of destruction of stockpiled chemicals and chemical weapons. However, quite often, due to non-observance of certain safety requirements and measures and the correct operation of equipment, accidents began to occur at chemical enterprises that caused human casualties among workers and negatively affected the environment, posing a threat to environmental safety. Previously, the Chemical Safety and Protection Program  was mentioned, which aims to promote and cooperate with representatives of the chemical industry to raise awareness among employees of enterprises about possible risks, threats, necessary requirements for equipment operation and much more. At the same time, despite all efforts, the risk of accidents at chemical plants remains high. First of all, this is due to the budget of the enterprise  —  some cannot allocate additional funds for timely repair or replacement of equipment. Cheap labor is also the Achilles' heel of some poor chemical plants, as these workers do not think much about the importance and necessity of compliance with requirements and safety measures, which also leads to industrial accidents. Based on the experience of past years, it is important to learn one lesson in this matter  —  cheap labor and savings on equipment are not a guarantee of successful production and accumulation of profits, but on the contrary  —  a chemical enterprise will lose even more resources  —  both workers and financial. In order to minimize the risks of this problem, it is necessary to abandon cheap labor in the person of emigrants, as well as people without special education and work experience, since they do not have basic skills in working with chemical materials and equipment, this is tantamount to a time bomb when an accident occurs. Most often, industrial accidents occur due to ignorance and inexperience of workers, or due to equipment malfunction. The situation is complicated by the fact that such savings are due to the fact that the national authorities unevenly distribute funding, appealing their decision by the fact that preference is given to those areas that, in their opinion, are in dire need of funding, while the chemical industry is being put into strict frameworks and restrictions. Thus, the OPCW, in cooperation with national authorities and the chemical industry, needs to think through a more detailed plan for funding programs, improve the personnel selection system for enterprises, continue to conduct seminars and workshops to exchange skills and knowledge, and motivate workers to properly comply with safety requirements at work. In this way, ensuring the CWC regime, among other things, presupposes the fight against threats and challenges arising in the course of the implementation of the CWC conditions. Given the fact that the interests of the participating states are shifting, politicization is observed in interaction with the chemical industry, which is expressed in the transition of the current issues being discussed to the discussion of political differences between the governments of the participating countries. For example, at the 25th Conference of the States Parties to the OPCW, discussions took place on the current issues of reporting on inspections and destruction of chemical weapons stockpiles, namely, that Russia completed the destruction of chemical weapons prematurely, but inspections continue, with the United States being the only State Party today which has chemical weapons . However, this issue was transferred to the topic of the case of the poisoning of Alexei Navalny, which, as a result, again led to another disagreement between the participating countries . It can be concluded that the internal political crisis of the OPCW has a negative impact on the provision of the CWC regime and also on interaction with the chemical industry, since due to political differences of the member states, the interests of the chemical industry are relegated to the background. The number of programs to support chemical enterprises will also decrease, since the problem of uneven distribution of funding exists not only at the national level, but also within the OPCW . 3.3. The future of the chemical industry in CWC regime The adoption of the Chemical Weapons Convention in 1997 imposed a number of obligations and restrictions on the chemical industry regarding the production and use of chemical materials. Despite the fact that the CWC brought chemical enterprises under the strict control of the member states, the terms of the Convention also provided for measures to switch the chemical industry from military production to non-military ones. In this regard, the chemical industry has gone through a certain period of restructuring and is still developing within the framework of the Chemical Weapons Convention. The development of the chemical industry is primarily associated with the introduction of new technologies and scientific developments to accelerate the pace of production, as well as quality, safety, and is aimed at generating greater profits. In this regard, the chemical industry can, in one way or another, be a participant in political processes at the national and international level, as evidenced by the participation of the chemical industry in the preparation of the verification system, which also required weaving into national legislation. At the same time, the most striking example of such involvement of representatives of the chemical industry in political processes was the participation of the chemical industry in meetings of the US Congress, where the adoption of the Chemical Weapons Convention was put on the agenda . When considering the issue of ensuring the CWC regime, representatives of the chemical industry act as an interested party, since the implementation of the conditions of the CWC presupposes the protection of the interests of the chemical industry. This applies to systems of verification and inspection, distribution of funding, exchange of personnel, scientific and technical achievements and information, which contributes to the development of lagging chemical industries in developing countries such as Latin America, the African Union and others. At the same time, there is an indirect influence of the chemical industry on the provision of the CWC regime. The introduction of new technologies in chemical production is a factor that has to break the previous system of inspections and production control. New technologies and equipment require higher qualifications from workers, accordingly, highly qualified experts are also required at the level of the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons. This is due to the fact that the OPCW, namely the OPCW Technical Secretariat, must provide technical support to all  States Parties, and this is associated with numerous programs for the exchange of personnel and technology between developed and developing countries to strengthen the overall system of international chemical safety. Based on the above and the contribution of the chemical industry representatives to the initial negotiations on the CWC, it can be concluded that the chemical industry can have both positive and negative impacts on the operation of the Convention.  It is important to note that there are no specific examples of negative consequences for the CWC regime from the interaction of the chemical industry and the OPCW in history. However, there is a judgment that a negative impact from the chemical industry is possible . Some researchers believe that the implementation of programs that include the participation of the chemical industry of countries that are not members of the CWC may lead to the fact that the chemical industry of these countries will have the necessary knowledge and qualifications to create chemical weapons, which may subsequently lead to re-emergence of chemical weapons . At the same time, the Director General of the OPCW Fernando Arias also notes that one of the main threats to ensuring the CWC regime in the future is “the revival of chemical weapons through the misuse of scientific and technological advances” . There is also a positive side of the influence of the chemical industry on ensuring the CWC regime. First of all, new technologies help to enhance the production efficiency of the Convention. In other words, new technologies apply not only to chemical production, but also give impetus to the development of verification systems and modernization of inspection equipment. Scientific advances allow updating the Schedules of Prohibited Chemicals, and deepening knowledge of their effect on the body and the environment, which also affects the effectiveness of the determination of chemical toxic substances, without waiting for the results of laboratory tests, and this leads to an acceleration of the system of rapid response to emergencies using chemical weapons or chemical agents. Another important point of the positive impact of the chemical industry is associated with a comprehensive and deeper analysis of gaps and shortcomings in the conditions of the Chemical Weapons Convention. As mentioned earlier, the governments of the participating States have a superficial, basic knowledge of the technical support of the CWC. The chemical industry will help governments look at problems and omissions from a different angle, so to speak, from within the CWC sphere of action, namely from the chemical industry point of view. In this way, the chemical industry can assist the OPCW in strengthening the CWC regime. Support and development programs, programs for adapting the conditions of the CWC, the exchange of professional personnel, information on current problems in production, on possible violations during inspections, on the introduction of new scientific and technical achievements into production between chemical enterprises of the CWC States Parties are only a small part of those steps from the side of the chemical industry, which help and will help to further strengthen the enforcement of the chemical weapons Convention regime. Today the CWC regime is going through difficult times. Cooperation between the chemical industry and States Parties through the OPCW plays a critical role in ensuring compliance with the provisions of the Chemical Weapons Convention. Interaction with the chemical industry to develop further steps of cooperation is slowing down due to the existing rather protracted political crisis  —  duplication of the UN Security Council's functions to determine the culprit in the use of chemical weapons  —  and a crisis of confidence within the Organization. However, despite all the challenges and threats, the OPCW has a completeness and sufficiency  activities as the organization to monitor the proper implementation of the conditions of the CWC. Although it should be noted that if the existing contradictions between the participating states further intensify the politicization of the OPCW and all processes related to the interaction of the OPCW with the chemical industry, this may lead to a weakening of the CWC regime, as well as negative consequences for the chemical industry. As the OPCW Director General Fernando Arias noted: “Today the OPCW faces several main tasks, including strengthening and expanding interaction with the chemical industry, as well as with representatives of NGOs, civil society, etc., and preventing the revival of chemical weapons, which is one of the main threats to the CWC regime” . In this way, the chemical industry is an important part for the future regime of the Chemical Weapons Convention, and the deepening of the interaction of the chemical industry with the OPCW makes it possible to protect its corporate interests at the international level  —  to a greater extent, this concerns the systems of inspection and verification of chemical enterprises, since the problem of maintaining a balance between the completeness of the information transmitted during inspection and confidentiality is one of the most relevant for the chemical industry  representatives. Conclusion The relationship between the Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW) and the chemical industry was not a given. Initially, the chemical industry did not play a big role, and the governments of the States Parties did not think about close cooperation with the chemical industry. However, when it came to the development of the Chemical Weapons Convention terms about the practical implementation of the CWC, the interests of the chemical industry and the national authorities were somewhat similar  —  each of the parties sought to protect their interests and to derive some benefits from this cooperation. Thus, the interaction of the chemical industry and national authorities within the OPCW has begun to evolve. Based on the history of the negotiation process, it can be concluded that these relations between the chemical industry and the OPCW have passed certain stages of development, which also pushed the chemical industry to develop  —  from a simple observer to close cooperation. In addition, representatives of the chemical industry have learned to clearly and structurally express their positions and views, and have mastered new tools for communicating with representatives of political power  —  written appeals, requests, policy briefs, policy memos, etc. At the same time, the deepening involvement of the chemical industry in the negotiation process has led to the fact that the chemical industry has formed national chemical associations to increase the effectiveness of cooperation with national authorities. In terms of reaching the international level, the national chemical industry associations have merged to form the International Council of Chemical Associations, which, in cooperation with the OPCW, covers verification, education, promotion and chemical safety activities at the international level. It is important to note that in this case, the chemical industry is considered not as an actor in international relations, but as a group of representatives of the chemical industry of the CWC member states, united to protect common interests  —  verification and inspection, maintaining the confidentiality of corporate information.  Also, the increased interaction between the chemical industry and the OPCW has led to the development of IT systems to ensure the information security of chemical enterprises and participating States. The introduction of new technologies in production gave an impetus to the development of the CWC and its adaptation to new realities. Today, the OPCW, as an international body for the implementation of the terms of the Convention, acts as a pledge of international guarantees for the protection of the interests of the entire chemical industry, and is also an international platform for the exchange of knowledge and experience between the chemical industry of developed and developing countries participating in the CWC. At the same time, the exchange includes both scientific and technical achievements, as well as international exchange, support, financing and development programs. In doing so, the OPCW is seen not only as an auditor and regulatory body, but also as a partner to improve the verification regime, which ensures that chemicals are not used for prohibited purposes, and to strengthen the capacity of States parties through international cooperation programmes. For the implementation of cooperation, exchange and development programs, there are certain communication channels between the chemical industry, national authorities and the OPCW. These communication channels have a clear structure and areas of activity - from solving financing issues to implementing scientific and technological achievements in production. The whole mechanism of interaction works effectively, which is clearly seen in the improvement of the level of development of the chemical industry in developing countries. The development of the chemical industry, as one of the main spheres of the economy of states, leads to an improvement in the level of development of the States themselves, primarily developing ones. This shows that the geopolitical situation in the world is changing, and the world is no longer the same as it was in 1997, when the Chemical Weapons Convention was adopted. Taking into account the changing realities of modern international relations, the problems raised at the initial negotiations were pushed aside, and new threats and challenges came to the fore. As a result, the following political problems that the chemical industry and the OPCW face when interacting with each other can be identified: International chemical terrorism Chemical safety and security Problems with financing Slowing down decision-making process on current issues Although the OPCW is experiencing a protracted political crisis, the activities of this Organization, primarily in the framework of interaction with representatives of the chemical industry, remain full and sufficient. New OPCW offices are being established in the territories of the developing countries participating in the CWC, which indicates the need for constant contact in view of the improved level of development of the chemical industry in developing countries. However, an important point is that the political problems in the interaction of the chemical industry and representatives of the OPCW are becoming more acute. The solution of these problems is necessary for the further development of cooperation between the OPCW and the chemical industry. By strengthening its role in the implementation of the CWC regime, the chemical industry can assist in finding solutions to the political problems presented. One of the possible solutions to the problems under consideration is to reduce the difference in the levels of development of the chemical industry in developing and developed States Parties to the CWC. This is possible if scientific and technical achievements are correctly implemented in production, which will improve the productivity of chemical facilities. Bringing to the national authorities reliable reports on the results of the use of new technologies by the chemical industry, which will list all the pros and cons, will help to refine these processes to practical implementation and mass use. The introduction of new scientific and technological advances in production will not only reduce costs but also strengthen safety systems, as well as bring the chemical industry facilities of all the CWC States Parties to a relatively equal level of development. This will provide an opportunity to develop and introduce uniform requirements for safety measures at work, which will reduce the risks of accidents. Today, the chemical industry and the OPCW representatives face an important task-to try to find a fine line so that the platforms of interaction and cooperation between the chemical industry and the OPCW are not used by the participating states to promote their geopolitical interests. In this way, working on the joint development projects listed above will reduce the level of tension between the States Parties, which will strengthen the regime of the Chemical Weapons Convention. Annexes Annex 1. Confidentiality Annex A. General Principles for the Handling of Confidential Information The obligation to protect confidential information shall pertain to the verification of both civil and military activities and facilities. Pursuant to the general obligations set forth in Article VIII, the Organization shall: Require only the minimum amount of information and data necessary for the timely and efficient carrying out of its responsibilities under this Convention; Take the necessary measures to ensure that inspectors and other staff members of the Technical Secretariat meet the highest standards of efficiency, competence, and integrity; (c) Develop agreements and regulations to implement the provisions of this Convention and shall specify as precisely as possible the information to which the Organization shall be given access by a State Party. The Director-General shall have the primary responsibility for ensuring the protection of confidential information. The Director-General shall establish a stringent regime governing the handling of confidential information by the Technical Secretariat, and in doing so, shall observe the following guidelines: Information shall be considered confidential if: (i) It is so designated by the State Party from which the information was obtained and to which the information refers; or (ii) In the judgement of the Director-General, its unauthorized disclosure could reasonably be expected to cause damage to the State Party to which it refers or to the mechanisms for implementation of this Convention; All data and documents obtained by the Technical Secretariat shall be evaluated by the appropriate unit of the Technical Secretariat in order to establish whether they contain confidential information. Data required by States Parties to be assured of the continued compliance with this Convention by other States Parties shall be routinely provided to them. Such data shall encompass: (i) The initial and annual reports and declarations provided by States Parties under Articles III, IV, V and VI, in accordance with the provisions set forth in the Verification Annex; (ii) General reports on the results and effectiveness of verification activities; and (iii) Information to be supplied to all States Parties in accordance with the provisions of this Convention; (c) No information obtained by the Organization in connection with the implementation of this Convention shall be published or otherwise released, except, as follows: (i) General information on the implementation of this Convention may be compiled and released publicly in accordance with the decisions of the Conference or the Executive Council; (ii) Any information may be released with the express consent of the State Party to which the information refers; (iii) Information classified as confidential shall be released by the Organization only through procedures which ensure that the release of information only occurs in strict conformity with the needs of this Convention. Such procedures shall be considered and approved by the Conference pursuant to Article VIII, paragraph 21 (i); (d) The level of sensitivity of confidential data or documents shall be established, based on criteria to be applied uniformly in order to ensure their appropriate handling and protection. For this purpose, a classification system shall be introduced, which by taking account of relevant work undertaken in the preparation of this Convention shall provide for clear criteria ensuring the inclusion of information into appropriate categories of confidentiality and the justified durability of the confidential nature of information. While providing for the necessary flexibility in its implementation the classification system shall protect the rights of States Parties providing confidential information. A classification system shall be considered and approved by the Conference pursuant to Article VIII, paragraph 21 (i); (e) Confidential information shall be stored securely at the premises of the Organization. Some data or documents may also be stored with the National Authority of a State Party. Sensitive information, including, inter alia, photographs, plans and other documents required only for the inspection of a specific facility may be kept under lock and key at this facility; (f) To the greatest extent consistent with the effective implementation of the verification provisions of this Convention, information shall be handled and stored by the Technical Secretariat in a form that precludes direct identification of the facility to which it pertains; (g) The amount of confidential information removed from a facility shall be kept to the minimum necessary for the timely and effective implementation of the verification provisions of this Convention; and (h) Access to confidential information shall be regulated in accordance with its classification. The dissemination of confidential information within the Organization shall be strictly on a need-to-know basis. The Director-General shall report annually to the Conference on the implementation of the regime governing the handling of confidential information by the Technical Secretariat. Each State Party shall treat information which it receives from the Organization in accordance with the level of confidentiality established for that information. Upon request, a State Party shall provide details on the handling of information provided to it by the Organization. B. Employment and Conduct of Personnel in the Technical Secretariat Conditions of staff employment shall be such as to ensure that access to and handling of confidential information shall be in conformity with the procedures established by the Director-General in accordance with Section A. Each position in the Technical Secretariat shall be governed by a formal position description that specifies the scope of access to confidential information, if any, needed in that position. The Director-General, the inspectors and the other members of the staff shall not disclose even after termination of their functions to any unauthorized persons any confidential information coming to their knowledge in the performance of their official duties. They shall not communicate to any State, organization or person outside the Technical Secretariat any information to which they have access in connection with their activities in relation to any State Party. In the discharge of their functions inspectors shall only request the information and data which are necessary to fulfil their mandate. They shall not make any records of information collected incidentally and not related to verification of compliance with this Convention. The staff shall enter into individual secrecy agreements with the Technical Secretariat covering their period of employment and a period of five years after it is terminated. In order to avoid improper disclosures, inspectors and staff members shall be appropriately advised and reminded about security considerations and of the possible penalties that they would incur in the event of improper disclosure. Not less than 30 days before an employee is given clearance for access to confidential information that refers to activities on the territory or in any other place under the jurisdiction or control of a State Party, the State Party concerned shall be notified of the proposed clearance. For inspectors the notification of a proposed designation shall fulfil this requirement. In evaluating the performance of inspectors and any other employees of the Technical Secretariat, specific attention shall be given to the employee’s record regarding protection of confidential information. C. Measures to Protect Sensitive Installations and Prevent Disclosure of Confidential Data in the Course of On-Site Verification Activities States Parties may take such measures as they deem necessary to protect confidentiality, provided that they fulfil their obligations to demonstrate compliance in accordance with the relevant Articles and the Verification Annex. When receiving an inspection, the State Party may indicate to the inspection team the equipment, documentation or areas that it considers sensitive and not related to the purpose of the inspection. Inspection teams shall be guided by the principle of conducting on-site inspections in the least intrusive manner possible consistent with the effective and timely accomplishment of their mission. They shall take into consideration proposals which may be made by the State Party receiving the inspection, at whatever stage of the inspection, to ensure that sensitive equipment or information, not related to chemical weapons, is protected. Inspection teams shall strictly abide by the provisions set forth in the relevant Articles and Annexes governing the conduct of inspections. They shall fully respect the procedures designed to protect sensitive installations and to prevent the disclosure of confidential data. In the elaboration of arrangements and facility agreements, due regard shall be paid to the requirement of protecting confidential information. Agreements on inspection procedures for individual facilities shall also include specific and detailed arrangements with regard to the determination of those areas of the facility to which inspectors are granted access, the storage of confidential information on-site, the scope of the inspection effort in agreed areas, the taking of samples and their analysis, the access to records and the use of instruments and continuous monitoring equipment. The report to be prepared after each inspection shall only contain facts relevant to compliance with this Convention. The report shall be handled in accordance with the regulations established by the Organization governing the handling of confidential information. If necessary, the information contained in the report shall be processed into less sensitive forms before it is transmitted outside the Technical Secretariat and the inspected State Party. D. Procedures in Case of Breaches or Alleged Breaches of Confidentiality The Director-General shall establish necessary procedures to be followed in case of breaches or alleged breaches of confidentiality, taking into account recommendations to be considered and approved by the Conference pursuant to Article VIII, paragraph 21 (i). The Director-General shall oversee the implementation of individual secrecy agreements. The Director-General shall promptly initiate an investigation if, in his judgement, there is sufficient indication that obligations concerning the protection of confidential information have been violated. The Director-General shall also promptly initiate an investigation if an allegation concerning a breach of confidentiality is made by a State Party. The Director-General shall impose appropriate punitive and disciplinary measures on staff members who have violated their obligations to protect confidential information. In cases of serious breaches, the immunity from jurisdiction may be waived by the Director-General. States Parties shall, to the extent possible, cooperate and support the Director-General in investigating any breach or alleged breach of confidentiality and in taking appropriate action in case a breach has been established. The Organization shall not be held liable for any breach of confidentiality committed by members of the Technical Secretariat. For breaches involving both a State Party and the Organization, a “Commission for the settlement of disputes related to confidentiality”, set up as a subsidiary organ of the Conference, shall consider the case. This Commission shall be appointed by the Conference. Rules governing its composition and operating procedures shall be adopted by the Conference at its first session. Цели:
1. Оценка эффективности мер контроля за экспортом химических веществ и биологических агентов.
2. Анализ практик безопасности и управления рисками в химической промышленности.

Задачи:
1. Изучить обновленные правила экспортных контрольных процедур на химические вещества и биологические агенты.
2. Оценить влияние международных соглашений на развитие национальных законодательных актов в области экспортного контроля.
3. Исследовать лучшие практики обеспечения информационной безопасности в химической промышленности.
4. Анализировать вызовы и перспективы развития Конвенции о запрещении химического оружия.
5. Оценить роль Организации по запрещению химического оружия в укреплении режима контроля над химическим оружием.
6. Исследовать опыт стран в области экспортного контроля за химическими веществами и биологическими агентами.
7. Анализировать влияние технологических инноваций на безопасность и управление рисками в химической промышленности.
8. Исследовать проблемы информационной безопасности в химической промышленности и способы их решения.
9. Оценить вклад международных организаций в развитие законодательства и практик экспортного контроля.
10. Исследовать влияние экологических катастроф на развитие законодательства и практик экспортного контроля.
